title: Glyphosate: Hepatotoxicity,
summary:
# Summary

Glyphosate and glyphosate-based herbicides (GBHs) function as endocrine disruptors with multifaceted mechanisms of reproductive toxicity, operating primarily through dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal and hypothalamic-pituitary-testicular axes, disruption of steroidogenesis, and induction of oxidative stress. Beyond their primary herbicidal action—competitive inhibition of EPSPS—these compounds cause gut microbiome dysbiosis and compromise the microbiota-gut-brain axis, while exhibiting hormone-mimicking properties that antagonize estrogen receptors and alter signal transduction in hormone-responsive cells. At the reproductive level, both glyphosate and Roundup severely impair male fertility by reducing sperm motility, causing DNA fragmentation, diminishing testosterone and spermatid numbers, and inducing Sertoli cell apoptosis through calcium-dependent oxidative stress. Exposure also triggers epigenetic modifications and transgenerational epimutations affecting development, manifesting in altered testicular morphology, delayed testicular descent, and abnormal masculinization patterns. Additionally, glyphosate-based herbicides cause hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, hypothyroidism, and osteoporosis through dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis, establishing a comprehensive endocrine and organ-system toxicity profile relevant to reproductive health and developmental disease pathogenesis.

title: The impact of sickle cell disease and its treatment on ovarian reserve in reproductive-aged Black women
summary: Sickle cell disease significantly impairs ovarian reserve in reproductive-aged Black women, with affected individuals showing approximately 50% lower anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) levels compared to controls, indicating reduced ovarian follicle pools. Multiple disease mechanisms contribute to this diminished reserve, including vaso-occlusive events that cause repetitive ischemic episodes in the ovaries and iron overload from frequent transfusions, which generates reactive oxygen species that trigger DNA damage, cell cycle arrest, and inhibition of granulosa cell proliferation. While hydroxyurea effectively treats sickle cell disease by reducing vaso-occlusive complications and decreasing inflammatory effects, current use of this agent paradoxically associates with 63% lower AMH values compared to non-users, though this may represent a transient effect on AMH secretion from granulosa cells. Disease severity correlates with genotype, with HbSS and HbS-Beta0 genotypes demonstrating the lowest AMH levels, while even milder HbSC genotypes show reduced ovarian reserve relative to healthy controls. These findings have important clinical implications, as women with sickle cell disease face a shortened reproductive window and diminished response to assisted reproductive technologies, necessitating careful counseling regarding fertility preservation and treatment planning.

title: Mesenchymal Stem Cells Ameliorate Cuprizone-Induced Demyelination by Targeting Oxidative Stress and Mitochondrial Dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) effectively ameliorate cuprizone-induced demyelination by counteracting oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction. Cuprizone, a copper chelator, impairs cytochrome oxidase activity and reduces oxidative phosphorylation, leading to oligodendrocyte damage and progressive demyelination. MSC transplantation reversed these pathological processes by upregulating antioxidant defenses, reducing the lipid peroxidation marker malondialdehyde while elevating glutathione and superoxide dismutase levels. Additionally, MSCs increased myelinated fiber density and oligodendrocyte numbers while suppressing harmful inflammatory responses including astrogliosis and microgliosis. The therapeutic mechanism involves MSC-mediated upregulation of key mitochondrial biogenesis regulators (PGC1α, NRF1, MFN2, and TFAM), restoring mitochondrial function and cellular energy production. These findings establish MSCs as a promising cell therapy for demyelinating diseases by directly targeting the oxidative stress-mitochondrial dysfunction axis that drives neurodegeneration.

title: The protective effects of fisetin in metabolic disorders: a focus on oxidative stress and associated events
journal: Journal of Diabetes and Metabolic Disorders
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that fisetin, a natural polyphenol compound, exerts protective effects against metabolic disorders primarily through activation of the Nrf2 antioxidant defense pathway. Oxidative stress is established as a critical driver of metabolic dysfunction, contributing to both insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome development. Fisetin counteracts these pathological processes by upregulating Nrf2, which serves as a master regulator of antioxidant defense and additionally modulates metabolic regulation and inflammatory responses. The compound shows therapeutic efficacy in treating diabetes and reducing both microvascular and macrovascular complications associated with metabolic disease. By enhancing cellular antioxidant capacity and suppressing inflammation while simultaneously promoting energy metabolism and cell survival, fisetin represents a promising therapeutic agent for improving overall metabolic health in conditions characterized by oxidative stress and metabolic dysfunction.

title: Cumulus cell expansion, nuclear maturation and embryonic development of bovine cumulus-oocyte complexes matured in varying concentrations of follicular fluid
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated how follicular fluid supplementation at varying concentrations affects bovine cumulus-oocyte complex maturation and embryonic development. Follicular fluid at 50% and 75% concentrations enhanced cumulus cell expansion, with 75% producing the greatest expansion, yet this improved expansion paradoxically did not translate to better embryo development outcomes. While 75% follicular fluid from small follicles consistently reduced blastocyst development and cleavage rates despite maximizing cumulus cell expansion, 50% supplementation achieved a beneficial balance by improving cumulus cell expansion while preserving oocyte competence and maintaining normal embryonic development. The findings reveal a critical disconnect between cumulus cell expansion and oocyte developmental competence, demonstrating that greater cumulus cell expansion does not guarantee improved embryonic outcomes. This research highlights the importance of optimizing follicular fluid concentration in in vitro maturation systems, as 50% represents an optimal threshold that enhances cumulus cell interactions without compromising the oocyte's capacity for successful fertilization and development.

title: 2024 AAOMPT Annual Conference Accepted Research Report and Case Report
journal: The Journal of Manual & Manipulative Therapy
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by elevated oxidative stress and chronic inflammation, which together drive disease pathogenesis through the excessive production of reactive oxygen species. The antioxidant defense mechanisms normally present in affected tissues are significantly compromised, leaving cells vulnerable to oxidative damage and contributing to the formation of ectopic endometrial lesions. This oxidative imbalance has been implicated in the infertility outcomes frequently observed in endometriosis patients. N-acetylcysteine, an antioxidant compound, has demonstrated therapeutic potential in reducing endometriosis symptoms in preclinical animal models, suggesting that pharmacological restoration of antioxidant capacity may represent a viable treatment strategy. These findings highlight oxidative stress as a central pathogenic mechanism in endometriosis and point toward antioxidant-based interventions as a promising avenue for managing both disease progression and associated reproductive dysfunction.

title: Does Resveratrol Impact Oxidative Stress Markers in Patients with Head and Neck Cancer Receiving Home Enteral Nutrition?
summary:
# Summary

This study investigated resveratrol's effects on oxidative stress markers in head and neck cancer patients receiving home enteral nutrition. Resveratrol supplementation significantly enhanced antioxidant defenses by upregulating key enzymes including superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase, while simultaneously reducing reactive oxygen species through inhibition of NADPH oxidase and activation of transcription factors such as Nrf-2 and SIRT1. The resveratrol group showed superior improvements in phase angle, a marker of cell membrane integrity and nutritional status, while preventing fat mass accumulation unlike the control group. Notably, despite these antioxidant benefits, malondialdehyde levels increased in the resveratrol group, suggesting the compound exhibits dual antioxidant and pro-oxidant activities. These findings indicate that resveratrol supplementation, particularly in liposomal formulations with enhanced bioavailability, may support cellular health and metabolic improvements in malnourished cancer patients undergoing enteral nutrition support.

title: Antioxidant Intake and Ovarian Reserve in Women Attending a Fertility Center
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress, characterized by elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) relative to antioxidant capacity, is a major driver of reproductive dysfunction including female infertility, granulosa cell apoptosis, and follicular atresia. Advanced maternal age exacerbates this imbalance by increasing ROS levels and reducing ovarian antioxidant content, leading to ovarian aging and diminished ovarian reserve. Antioxidant intake, particularly vitamins A, C, and E along with carotenoids such as lycopene, counteracts oxidative stress by scavenging free radicals and improving ovarian function. Clinical evidence demonstrates that combined vitamin C and E supplementation improves endometriosis outcomes and endometriosis-associated infertility, while lycopene shows particular promise in protecting granulosa cells and follicles through activation of the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway and prevention of follicle depletion. Additionally, lycopene intake is positively associated with antral follicle count and ovarian reserve in fertility patients, with tomato-based products serving as a primary dietary source. These findings suggest that optimizing dietary antioxidant intake, especially lycopene and vitamin supplementation, may represent a therapeutic strategy to mitigate oxidative stress-related infertility in conditions including endometriosis and premature ovarian insufficiency.

title: The Novel Imipramine–Magnesium Complex Exerts Antidepressant-like Activity in Mice Subjected to the Forced Swim Test and the Tail Suspension Test
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that a novel imipramine–magnesium complex exhibits potent antidepressant-like effects in mice using behavioral models of depression, with the 5 mg/kg dose achieving efficacy comparable to 30 mg/kg imipramine alone. The antidepressant activity involves magnesium's modulation of multiple neurotransmitter systems including NMDA, serotonergic, dopaminergic, and noradrenergic receptors, as well as regulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis. The complex acts synergistically through enhanced antioxidant mechanisms, particularly by increasing glutathione reductase activity and total antioxidant status, counteracting the oxidative stress that contributes to depression pathogenesis. Notably, the imipramine–magnesium combination addresses a critical mechanism whereby magnesium deficiency promotes depression by increasing reactive oxygen species and nitric oxide production. This fixed-dose combination format offers practical advantages including reduced pill burden and improved patient compliance while maintaining efficacy at lower doses than either component alone, positioning it as a promising therapeutic approach for depression management.

title: Reproductive Toxicity of Zearalenone and Its Molecular Mechanisms: A Review
summary:
# Summary

Zearalenone (ZEA), a mycotoxin produced by *Fusarium graminearum* and found in contaminated cereals, exerts widespread reproductive toxicity through multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on oxidative stress and endocrine disruption. ZEA competitively binds estrogen receptors (particularly α and β subtypes), disrupting endocrine function and triggering inflammation, hypertrophy, and cyst formation in reproductive organs while reducing germ cell viability and inducing apoptosis in both sexes. The toxin generates reactive oxygen species that overwhelm cellular antioxidant defenses—including T-SOD, GSH-Px, and catalase—leading to DNA damage, mitochondrial dysfunction, and elevated lipid peroxidation markers. While ZEA activates the protective Nrf2 signaling pathway and autophagy-related gene expression, these responses are insufficient to counteract its pro-apoptotic effects, evidenced by upregulation of Caspase-3 and downregulation of Bcl-2 in testicular cells. Clinical consequences include impaired male fertility (reduced sperm count and motility), female reproductive dysfunction (abortion, follicular abnormalities), altered hormone levels, and increased risk of hormone-dependent malignancies, making ZEA a significant reproductive health threat through dietary exposure.

title: A Retrospective Cohort Study Investigating the Effect of Intraovarian Platelet-Rich Plasma Therapy on the Oxidative State of Follicular Fluid in Women with Diminished Ovarian Reserve
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress significantly impairs fertility in women with diminished ovarian reserve by damaging oocytes, embryos, and reducing pregnancy outcomes through mechanisms involving reactive oxygen species-induced DNA damage and genetic changes. Intraovarian platelet-rich plasma therapy effectively counteracts this oxidative burden by upregulating antioxidant enzymes (catalase and superoxide dismutase) and total antioxidant capacity while simultaneously reducing oxidative markers including total oxidant status and malondialdehyde in follicular fluid. Beyond its antioxidant effects, PRP treatment elevated circulating anti-müllerian hormone and estradiol levels, key indicators of ovarian reserve and fertility potential. Following a single PRP injection, patients demonstrated notable improvements in oocyte quantity and quality, embryo development, and biochemical pregnancy rates, suggesting the therapy's multifaceted mechanism of action. These findings indicate that PRP represents a promising adjuvant treatment strategy for restoring ovarian function and improving in vitro fertilization outcomes in women with diminished ovarian reserve by simultaneously reducing oxidative damage and enhancing reproductive hormone signaling.

title: Ameliorative Role of β‐Caryophyllene on Antioxidant Biomarkers in a Paroxetine‐Induced Model of Male Sexual Dysfunction
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that β-caryophyllene (β-CBP), a sesquiterpene with potent antioxidant properties, effectively treats paroxetine-induced male sexual dysfunction by restoring oxidative balance in penile tissue. Paroxetine-induced dysfunction operates through elevated reactive oxygen species that inhibit critical enzymes—nitric oxide synthase and phosphodiesterase type 5—which are required for penile blood flow regulation and smooth muscle relaxation. β-CBP counteracts this pathology through multiple mechanisms: it directly scavenges free radicals (DPPH, ABTS, and hydroxyl radicals), chelates iron to prevent lipid peroxidation, and upregulates antioxidant enzymes including superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione peroxidase, and glutathione S-transferase in both penile and liver tissues. Additionally, β-CBP binds to and upregulates CB2 cannabinoid receptors in penile tissue, enhancing cannabinoid-mediated signaling pathways involved in erectile function, while also increasing testosterone levels to support sexual performance. These integrated antioxidant and receptor-mediated effects restore erectile function and reproductive health in the paroxetine-induced dysfunction model.

title: Eye on the horizon: The metabolic landscape of the RPE in aging and disease
journal: Progress in retinal and eye research
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the metabolic landscape of the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) across aging and disease, with emphasis on how metabolic dysregulation contributes to age-related macular degeneration. The RPE, a specialized monolayer that forms the outer blood-retina barrier, manages critical functions including bidirectional nutrient transport, photoreceptor outer segment phagocytosis, and waste product clearance. Photoreceptors rely heavily on glucose metabolism through both aerobic glycolysis and oxidative phosphorylation to meet their extraordinary energy demands, while the RPE preferentially fuels itself through oxidative phosphorylation and lactate consumption. During aging, the RPE experiences escalating oxidative stress due to mitochondrial dysfunction, declining antioxidant enzyme activity, and accumulation of damaging products such as lipofuscin and advanced glycation end-products, accompanied by elevated inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. The RPE maintains metabolic homeostasis through specialized pathways including fatty acid β-oxidation, amino acid metabolism (particularly serine and proline), cholesterol homeostasis, and the pentose phosphate pathway, which generates NADPH for antioxidant defenses. Perturbations in this delicate metabolic balance during aging—including increased Bruch's membrane stiffness and drusen formation—establish chronic inflammatory conditions that drive disease pathogenesis.

title: Mechanisms of oxidative stress-induced sperm dysfunction
summary:
# Oxidative Stress-Induced Sperm Dysfunction Summary

Oxidative stress is a major contributor to male infertility, primarily through excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) production that damages critical sperm components. ROS initiates lipid peroxidation of the sperm membrane rich in polyunsaturated fatty acids, generating toxic byproducts like malondialdehyde and 4-hydroxynonenal that reduce membrane fluidity and impair motility. Additionally, ROS causes protein oxidation of cytoskeletal elements such as actin and tubulin, and induces both single-strand and double-strand DNA breaks that compromise paternal genome integrity and are strongly correlated with infertility. Key sources of pathological ROS include leukocyte activation during infection or inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction during energy production, and environmental exposures such as smoking and heavy metal contamination. Although physiological ROS levels are essential for normal sperm capacitation, acrosome reaction, and fertilization, excessive ROS overwhelms endogenous antioxidant defenses including superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase. This review establishes oxidative stress as a central mechanistic pathway in male infertility within the context of endometriosis-related reproductive failure.

title: Redox profiling of preovulatory follicular fluid in the donkey is species-specific, and contributes to modulate sperm function
summary:
# Summary

Preovulatory follicular fluid (PFF) in donkeys contains a species-specific redox profile characterized by elevated glutathione peroxidase (GPX) and paraoxonase-1 (PON-1) activity alongside lower levels of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and total antioxidant capacity markers compared to other mammals. When donkey sperm are exposed to PFF, intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) and superoxide levels are significantly reduced, which protects against oxidative damage and maintains sperm motility, viability, and kinematic parameters including velocity and beat pattern. The study demonstrates that PFF fills the oviduct following ovulation and exerts protective effects against ROS-induced sperm DNA damage, complementing the ROS-controlling function of seminal plasma in the reproductive tract. These findings indicate that the antioxidant composition of follicular fluid is species-specific and plays a critical role in supporting sperm function during fertilization, with implications for understanding oxidative stress in reproductive pathologies including endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Methylglyoxal Formation—Metabolic Routes and Consequences
summary:
# Summary

Methylglyoxal (MGO), a toxic byproduct of glycolysis, ketone body metabolism, and lipid metabolism, is a potent glycotoxin that drives oxidative stress through multiple pathways central to endometriosis-related pathology. MGO accumulates during cellular metabolism and generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) while simultaneously activating pro-inflammatory signaling cascades including NF-κB, MAPK, and Nrf2 pathways, ultimately triggering apoptosis and endoplasmic reticulum stress. The primary cellular defense against MGO accumulation involves the glyoxalase system, where glyoxalase 1 and 2 convert MGO to D-lactate through glutathione conjugation, though overexpression of these enzymes paradoxically promotes cancer progression and therapy resistance. AGEs themselves perpetuate oxidative stress by stimulating NADPH oxidase and superoxide radical production while simultaneously damaging DNA and RNA through covalent interactions, processes implicated in aging and neurodegenerative pathology. Genetic variations in detoxification enzymes such as aldose reductase and ALDH are associated with diabetic complications and various systemic diseases, highlighting the critical role of MGO metabolism in disease pathogenesis. These interconnected metabolic mechanisms establish MGO accumulation as a key driver of the oxidative stress environment characteristic of endometriosis and associated infertility.

title: Unveiling the Impact of COVID-19 on Ovarian Function and Premature Ovarian Insufficiency: A Systematic Review
summary: SARS-CoV-2 infection impairs ovarian function and causes premature ovarian insufficiency through multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on oxidative stress, inflammation, and immunological dysfunction. The virus enters ovarian tissues via ACE2 receptors expressed on granulosa and cumulus cells, where it generates excessive reactive oxygen species and activates the NLRP3 inflammasome, triggering pyroptosis and impairing cellular homeostasis. This pathological cascade elevates pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6, TNF-α, and C-reactive protein while reducing key ovarian reserve markers such as AMH and increasing FSH levels, collectively compromising oocyte quality and maturation. Additionally, SARS-CoV-2 infection induces anti-ovarian antibodies that further damage ovarian function, and anti-viral IgG antibodies detected in follicular fluid correlate with altered lipid metabolism and diminished MII oocyte production. The cumulative effects result in reduced antral follicle count, decreased estradiol production, and compromised oocyte competence, establishing COVID-19 as a significant risk factor for reproductive dysfunction in affected women.

title: Immunomodulatory Effects of Selected Non-Nutritive Bioactive Compounds and Their Role in Optimal Nutrition
summary:
# Summary

This review examines how selected macronutrients and bioactive compounds modulate immune function and oxidative stress, with direct implications for conditions involving chronic inflammation like endometriosis-related infertility. Magnesium deficiency emerges as a key contributor to oxidative stress and inflammation, while potassium plays a critical role by preventing NALP3 inflammasome activation and subsequent pro-inflammatory cytokine release; conversely, elevated sodium promotes macrophage migration and cytokine production, disrupting gut microbiota and exacerbating inflammation. Essential trace elements including zinc, selenium, and iron are crucial for T and B lymphocyte function, natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and antibody production, with their deficiencies consistently impairing immune responses and increasing inflammatory markers. Bioactive compounds such as omega-3 fatty acids, sodium butyrate, lipoic acid, and coenzyme Q10 provide anti-inflammatory and antioxidant protection, while probiotics and prebiotics modulate the intestinal microbiota to maintain barrier integrity and reduce systemic inflammation. Together, these nutritional interventions target the oxidative stress and immune dysregulation characteristic of endometriosis, suggesting that optimized mineral, trace element, and bioactive compound status may improve fertility outcomes by reducing pathological inflammation.

title: The Potential of Superoxide Dismutase-Rich Tetraselmis chuii as a Promoter of Cellular Health
summary:
# Summary

Superoxide dismutase-rich *Tetraselmis chuii* (SOD-rich T. chuii), a microalgae species, functions as an indirect antioxidant by upregulating intracellular antioxidant enzymes including SOD, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase while simultaneously activating the NRF2 and SIRT1 signaling pathways that enhance cellular stress responses. The microalgae metabolites promote anti-inflammatory effects through downregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNFα) and upregulation of anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-10, which inhibits pro-inflammatory secretion and promotes tissue repair. Additionally, SOD-rich T. chuii enhances mitochondrial function by upregulating genes associated with mitochondrial biogenesis and ATP synthesis, thereby improving cellular energy production and physical performance in preclinical and clinical models. In the context of endometriosis-related infertility, these antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms are particularly relevant given that oxidative stress damages cellular constituents (proteins, lipids, DNA) and impairs spermatogenesis and reproductive function. The compound's multifaceted bioactivity—including immunostimulatory effects on complement activity, phagocytosis, and intestinal barrier integrity—suggests potential therapeutic applications for managing oxidative stress-driven pathologies affecting reproduction and cellular health.

title: Effects of dietary quercetin on retrieved mouse oocytes and
summary:
# Summary

This study examined dietary quercetin supplementation in mice and its effects on oocyte retrieval and embryo development. While quercetin possesses well-established antioxidant properties—scavenging free radicals, inhibiting lipid peroxidation, and upregulating antioxidant enzymes including superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase—dietary supplementation unexpectedly reduced embryo quality by decreasing blastocyst cell numbers, trophectoderm cells, and hatching rates without improving oocyte retrieval or early developmental parameters. The authors attribute this detrimental effect to excessive ROS suppression disrupting normal cellular redox balance in mice without oxidative stress; since low levels of ROS are essential for cell function, growth, and controlled cell death, over-suppression paradoxically induces pro-oxidant activity and impairs reproductive outcomes. These findings suggest that antioxidant supplementation should be reserved for conditions with specific oxidative stress risks, such as endometriosis and polycystic ovarian syndrome, rather than applied universally to normal physiological states.

title: Proteomic analysis reveals the alleviation of follicular development defects in offspring mice under DEHP exposure by melatonin
summary:
# Summary

This proteomic study demonstrates that melatonin alleviates follicular development defects in offspring mice exposed to the endocrine disruptor DEHP (diethyl phthalate). DEHP causes reproductive damage through multiple mechanisms: it induces oxidative stress and DNA damage in oocytes, damages gap junctions critical for follicle formation, and disrupts steroidogenesis by upregulating genes like Scarb1, Star, and Cyp17a1 while reducing 17β-estradiol production through competitive binding of its metabolite MEHP to HSD17B2. Melatonin reverses these DEHP-induced defects by functioning as a potent antioxidant that removes reactive oxygen species, promotes DNA repair, and competitively displaces MEHP from HSD17B2 to restore estradiol synthesis. The melatonin rescue significantly restored primordial follicles, antral follicles, and oocyte numbers while enhancing granulosa cell proliferation, mitochondrial function, and gap junction communication. These findings establish melatonin as a therapeutic approach to mitigate DEHP-induced reproductive toxicity and the associated oxidative stress-driven infertility implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Increased proline intake during gestation alleviates obesity-related impaired fetal development and placental function in gilts
summary:
# Summary

Maternal obesity impairs fetal development and placental function through reduced proline availability and increased oxidative stress, but dietary proline supplementation effectively reverses these detrimental effects. Proline enhances placental nutrient transport, vascular development, and angiogenesis, while reducing mummified fetuses and low-birth-weight piglets in obese pregnant gilts. The beneficial effects of proline are mediated through the sirtuin 1–superoxide dismutase 2–mitochondrial reactive oxygen species homeostatic axis, which counteracts the lipotoxicity and oxidative stress associated with maternal obesity. Increased dietary proline intake significantly increased piglet birth weight, litter weight, and the number of normal-birth-weight piglets while substantially reducing adverse pregnancy outcomes including intrauterine growth restriction. Placental proline levels showed strong positive correlations with piglet birth weight and vitality, and negative correlations with maternal body mass index and low-birth-weight incidence, establishing proline as a key nutrient for supporting reproductive success in obese mothers. These findings highlight amino acid supplementation, particularly proline, as a potential therapeutic strategy to improve gestational outcomes and fetal health in pregnancies complicated by maternal obesity.

title: Single-cell dual-omics reveals translational and transcriptional landscapes and regulations in oocytes from ovarian endometriosis patients
summary:
# Summary

This study employs single-cell dual-omics analysis to elucidate how ovarian endometriosis (OE) compromises oocyte quality through translational and transcriptional dysregulation. The researchers discovered that OE dramatically alters the translational landscape of germinal vesicle-stage oocytes, with over 2,400 genes showing differential translational efficiency compared to only 505 genes with altered transcription, highlighting that the translatome is a more sensitive indicator of oocyte dysfunction than the transcriptome. Three critical pathways were identified as driving OE-induced infertility: oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species dysregulation, meiotic cell cycle dysfunction (involving genes like CCNB1, CDK1, CHEK1, and AURKB), and RNA splicing dysregulation mediated by elevated translational efficiency of splicing factors such as SNRPB and HNRNPA1. Notably, OE-affected oocytes exhibit compensatory upregulation of autophagy and mitophagy pathways alongside increased cell cycle arrest, DNA repair dysfunction, and apoptosis, suggesting a maladaptive cellular response to oxidative damage. These findings reveal that post-transcriptional mechanisms, particularly translational control and alternative splicing dysregulation, represent novel molecular pathways underlying endometriosis-related infertility and may offer new therapeutic targets.

title: Chlorogenic Acid Ameliorates Chronic Unpredictable Stress‐Induced Diminished Ovarian Reserve Through Ovarian Renin‐Angiotensin System
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that chronic unpredictable stress (CUS) impairs ovarian reserve through hyperactivation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, which subsequently overactivates the ovarian renin-angiotensin system (OVRAS), specifically the ACE-AngII-AT1R pathway. This overactivation increases oxidative stress and triggers granulosa cell apoptosis, leading to diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) characterized by reduced antral follicles, elevated FSH levels, and decreased estradiol. Chlorogenic acid (CGA) ameliorates stress-induced DOR by inhibiting ACE activity, downregulating the ACE-AngII-AT1R axis, and reducing oxidative stress and glucocorticoid-induced mitochondrial dysfunction. CGA treatment restored ovarian function, normalized hormonal parameters (CORT, AMH, FSH), recovered estrous cyclicity, and increased the numbers of primordial, secondary, and mature follicles in stressed mice. These findings reveal that the OVRAS represents a key mechanistic link between stress-induced HPA dysregulation and ovarian dysfunction, and identify CGA as a potential therapeutic agent for stress-related infertility.

title: Bacteroides Fragilis Transplantation Reverses Reproductive Senescence by Transporting Extracellular Vesicles Through the Gut‐Ovary Axis
summary:
# Summary

Bacteroides fragilis transplantation ameliorates ovarian aging by secreting extracellular vesicles (EVs) enriched with microRNA-1246 (miR-1246) that travel through the gut-ovary axis to reduce oxidative stress in aging oocytes. miR-1246 targets the E3 ligase SKP2 to stabilize p62 protein, which then binds Keap1 and dissociates the Keap1-Nrf2 complex, allowing Nrf2 nuclear translocation and activation of antioxidant enzyme transcription including SOD1, SOD2, and CAT. BF treatment significantly improves ovarian function in aging mice by reducing reactive oxygen species and restoring mitochondrial ATP production and mtDNA copy numbers, while improving follicle development, oocyte quality, and embryonic developmental capacity. The therapeutic effects are entirely dependent on miR-1246 in the EVs, as demonstrated by knockout and knockdown studies showing that loss of miR-1246 abolishes BF-EV benefits. These findings reveal a novel microbiota-mediated mechanism linking gut dysbiosis to reproductive senescence through oxidative stress pathways, offering a potential probiotic intervention strategy for age-related infertility.

title: Altered Mitochondrial Morphology and Reduced Cardiolipin Levels in Oocytes of Endometriosis Model Mice: Implications for Mitochondrial Dysfunction in Infertility
journal: Medical Science Monitor: International Medical Journal of Experimental and Clinical Research
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis causes significant mitochondrial dysfunction in oocytes through alterations in cardiolipin metabolism. In an endometriosis mouse model, oocytes showed an 87.5% reduction in intact mitochondrial morphology and markedly decreased cardiolipin levels compared to controls, with a strong positive correlation (r=0.73) between these two parameters. The inflammatory milieu of endometriosis—characterized by elevated TNF-α and oxidative stress—triggers mitochondrial damage through multiple pathways: TNF-α activation induces reactive oxygen species generation and permeability transition pores, while lipoxygenase activity directly oxidizes cardiolipin, compromising the inner mitochondrial membrane's structural and functional integrity. Cardiolipin reduction impairs critical mitochondrial functions including ATP synthesis, oxidative phosphorylation, and mitochondrial dynamics, collectively impairing oocyte quality and maturation. These findings establish mitochondrial cardiolipin loss as a key mechanistic link between endometriosis-associated inflammation, oxidative stress, and reduced fertility, suggesting cardiolipin preservation could represent a therapeutic target for endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Follicle-stimulating hormone stimulates free radical generation without inducing substantial oxidative stress in human granulosa cells
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) stimulates robust free radical generation in human granulosa cells by activating the FSH receptor and promoting mitochondrial respiration, yet this elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) production does not cause substantial oxidative damage to cellular DNA, proteins, or lipids. FSH induces dose-independent increases in both cytoplasmic and mitochondrial ROS levels in cumulus and mural granulosa cells, with mitochondrial ROS changes being more pronounced than cytoplasmic changes. Despite elevated free radical loads, FSH treatment does not compromise granulosa cell viability or cause oxidative damage, likely because ROS production remains balanced within physiological thresholds where ROS can function as beneficial signaling molecules essential for folliculogenesis, oocyte maturation, and ovulation. However, excessive or supraphysiological FSH exposure may disrupt this balance, as elevated cumulus granulosa cell ROS particularly increases under high FSH concentrations, potentially contributing to reduced oocyte quality. The findings suggest that while physiological FSH-induced ROS is necessary and benign, the concern with high-dose FSH stimulation protocols in assisted reproduction relates to excessive free radical accumulation rather than FSH's inherent ability to generate ROS.

title: Predictive value of homocysteine levels in embryo culture media for embryo selection in infertile patients with endometriosis
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: Endometriosis is strongly associated with infertility through multiple mechanisms, including impaired oocyte quality and elevated oxidative stress. The study demonstrates that homocysteine, a metabolite involved in glutathione production and antioxidant defense, is significantly elevated in embryo culture media of endometriosis patients compared to controls, with levels of 4.31±0.48 µmol/L versus lower values in non-endometriosis patients. Elevated homocysteine levels cause reduced cell division, increased embryo fragmentation, and diminished oocyte quality, collectively impairing embryo viability and development. Notably, homocysteine levels in embryo culture media were substantially lower in patients who achieved successful pregnancy (3.60±0.84 µmol/L), suggesting independent predictive value for pregnancy outcomes. These findings indicate that homocysteine quantification in embryo culture media represents a promising metabolomic biomarker for embryo selection and pregnancy prediction in infertile endometriosis patients, offering a non-invasive assessment tool to guide clinical decision-making in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Astaxanthin Alleviates Oxidative Stress in Mouse Preantral Follicles and Enhances Follicular Development Through the AMPK Signaling Pathway
summary: Astaxanthin, a potent natural carotenoid, enhances in vitro development of mouse preantral follicles by alleviating oxidative stress through activation of the AMPK signaling pathway. At a concentration of 2.5 nM, astaxanthin significantly increased antrum formation rates (85.96% to 94.38%), oocyte maturation rates (79.15% to 85.12%), follicle attachment area, and estradiol secretion while simultaneously reducing reactive oxygen species and malondialdehyde levels. The compound's protective effects are mediated through AMPK activation, which enhances expression of downstream antioxidant genes including glutathione and superoxide dismutase 1, while promoting mitochondrial biogenesis and function via upregulation of PGC-1α, NRF1, TFAM, and electron transport chain components. Astaxanthin additionally suppressed follicular apoptosis by improving mitochondrial membrane potential and decreasing pro-apoptotic markers including cleaved caspase 3, Bax, and p53. Pharmacological AMPK inhibition completely reversed astaxanthin's beneficial effects on oxidative stress reduction and follicular development, confirming that the AMPK pathway is essential for mediating this compound's protective action on oocyte quality and folliculogenesis.

title: Impact of Vegan and Vegetarian Diets on Neurological Health: A Critical Review
summary:
# Summary

Well-planned vegan and vegetarian diets reduce systemic inflammation and oxidative stress by providing abundant phytonutrients and antioxidants, including polyphenols, flavonoids, and carotenoids that scavenge free radicals and lower inflammatory markers such as C-reactive protein and interleukin-6. These diets also promote beneficial gut bacteria proliferation and butyrate production, which strengthens the blood-brain barrier and reduces neuroinflammation, potentially lowering risk for neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's. However, plant-based diets carry nutritional risks that must be carefully managed, including deficiencies in vitamin B12, omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA), iron, and other minerals—deficiencies linked to cognitive decline, mood disturbances, myelin sheath degeneration, and elevated homocysteine levels. Additionally, phytates and polyphenols in plant foods inhibit mineral bioavailability, while lectins may trigger gastrointestinal dysfunction and systemic inflammation if not properly prepared. The neurological benefits of these diets depend critically on strategic supplementation and food preparation to mitigate nutrient absorption challenges while maximizing the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant advantages.

title: Enhancing gut-ovary health in aged laying hens: the impact of dietary betaine supplementation
summary:
# Dietary Betaine Supplementation Enhances Reproductive Performance in Aged Laying Hens Through Gut-Ovarian Axis Optimization

Dietary betaine supplementation, particularly at higher doses (H-Bet), significantly improves laying performance and egg production in aged laying hens by simultaneously enhancing intestinal barrier integrity and mitigating oxidative stress in reproductive tissues. High-dose betaine strengthens the intestinal epithelium by upregulating tight junction proteins (claudins, occludin, and ZO-1) and increasing villus height while reducing crypt depth, thereby maintaining barrier function critical for nutrient absorption and preventing bacterial translocation during aging. The compound modulates the gut microbiota composition by enriching beneficial Bacteroidetes and reducing the Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio, which supports intestinal homeostasis and reproductive function through microbial metabolite production. Betaine activates multiple antioxidant defense pathways in both the jejunal mucosa and ovary, upregulating superoxide dismutase, catalase, and the Nrf2-mediated antioxidant response system (NQO-1 and HO-1), thereby counteracting age-related reactive oxygen species accumulation and ovarian senescence. Transcriptome analysis of developing follicles revealed that betaine upregulates ATP6, enhancing mitochondrial energy production while downregulating pro-inflammatory EGR1, supporting oocyte quality and reproductive longevity in aging hens.

title: Exploration of the mechanism and therapy of ovarian aging by targeting cellular senescence
summary:
# Summary

This review examines cellular senescence as a central mechanism driving ovarian aging and reduced fertility. Multiple pathways converge to trigger senescence in ovarian cell types including oocytes, granulosa cells, theca cells, and endothelial cells: oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, DNA damage, telomere shortening, and advanced glycation end products all promote this process. Telomerase activity naturally declines with age in the ovary, and reduced expression of telomerase-associated proteins (TERT, TRF1, TRF2, POT1a) correlates with primordial follicle depletion and oocyte dysfunction, suggesting telomerase activation may delay senescence. DNA repair pathway dysfunction, particularly impaired BRCA1/2 and ATM-mediated double-strand break repair, plays a critical role in oocyte senescence and premature menopause, especially in BRCA-mutated individuals. Oxidative stress drives ovarian aging by generating reactive oxygen species that damage telomeres, disrupt meiotic spindles, and impair folliculogenesis and ovulation, while ovarian antioxidant capacity declines with age. Therapeutic approaches targeting these mechanisms—such as telomerase activation, oxidative stress reduction via antioxidants like ketoglutaric acid, and enhancement of DNA repair—offer promise for mitigating age-related fertility decline and associated clinical symptoms.

title: The effect of endometriosis on oocyte quality: mechanisms, diagnosis and treatment
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis impairs fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms that compromise oocyte quality and quantity. The disease reduces follicle count, oocyte maturation rates, fertilization rates, and blastocyst formation, while increasing spontaneous abortion and fetal aneuploidy rates. A key pathological mechanism involves elevated reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the follicular microenvironment, which causes spindle instability, chromosome damage, telomere shortening, and mitochondrial dysfunction with reduced mtDNA copy number in oocytes. Peritoneal fluid from endometriosis patients directly induces microtubule and chromosome damage in oocytes, while altered granulosa cell function and dysregulated cytokine signaling (including elevated IL-6, IL-8, and IL-12) further compromise oocyte development. Antioxidant treatment can reverse ROS-mediated damage and prevent chromosomal abnormalities, while surgical intervention and oocyte donation from healthy donors improve reproductive outcomes, suggesting that targeting oxidative stress and the abnormal follicular microenvironment represents a therapeutic strategy in endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Effects of Oxidative Stress and Antioxidant Activity in Plasma and Uterine Fluid During Early Postpartum on Subsequent Reproductive Performance of Japanese Black Cows
journal: Animals : an Open Access Journal from MDPI
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that oxidative stress levels during early postpartum significantly affect subsequent reproductive performance in Japanese Black cattle, with higher oxidation stress indices in uterine fluid associated with poor pregnancy outcomes and delayed conception. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) impair critical reproductive processes including oocyte maturation, fertilization, and embryonic development, while postpartum inflammatory conditions such as metritis and endometritis perpetuate oxidative stress and compromise the uterine environment necessary for pregnancy establishment. The research reveals that antioxidant concentrations in uterine fluid improve fertility outcomes, and a proper balance between ROS and antioxidants is essential for successful reproduction. Notably, oxidative stress is implicated not only in reproductive failure but also in gynecologic diseases including endometriosis, suggesting common pathophysiological mechanisms across reproductive disorders. The findings indicate that measuring oxidation stress index and antioxidant activity in the early postpartum period may serve as useful indicators of reproductive capacity, and antioxidant supplementation—potentially including agents like nobiletin with anti-inflammatory properties—could represent a therapeutic approach to improve fertility in cattle with elevated intrauterine oxidative stress.

title: miR-361-5p regulates SLC25A24 to maintain mitochondrial function and alleviate granulosa cell dysfunction in diminished ovarian reserve
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that miR-361-5p, a tumor suppressor microRNA, maintains granulosa cell function and alleviates dysfunction in diminished ovarian reserve (DOR) by negatively regulating SLC25A24, a mitochondrial solute carrier. MiR-361-5p expression is significantly reduced in DOR patients' granulosa cells and inversely correlates with FSH levels while positively correlating with anti-müllerian hormone (AMH) levels and antral follicle count. MiR-361-5p overexpression enhances granulosa cell viability, increases steroidogenic hormone production (estradiol and progesterone), and improves mitochondrial function by reducing reactive oxygen species levels while increasing ATP production and mitochondrial membrane potential. Conversely, miR-361-5p inhibition decreases cell viability, promotes apoptosis, and impairs mitochondrial homeostasis, effects that are reversed by SLC25A24 knockdown. These findings establish miR-361-5p as a key regulator protecting granulosa cells from oxidative stress-induced dysfunction and mitochondrial impairment in DOR.

title: Protective effects of corn silk and asparagus Officinalis against formaldehyde-induced reproductive toxicity in male rats via CDK2/Spem1/Fbxo47 and Tet1 pathways
summary: Formaldehyde exposure causes severe reproductive toxicity in male rats by downregulating critical fertility genes (CDK2, Spem1, Fbxo47, and Tet1) and inducing oxidative stress, resulting in reduced testicular weight, testosterone levels, sperm count, motility, and morphology. The toxic effects involve hormonal imbalances, seminiferous epithelium alterations, cell atrophy, and excessive autophagy-mediated cellular degradation in testicular tissues. Treatment with corn silk and asparagus officinalis extracts, rich in phenolic compounds (gallic acid and syringic acid respectively), effectively reversed formaldehyde-induced reproductive damage by restoring antioxidant status, reducing inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, and improving weight gain and testicular recovery. These plant extracts restored reproductive hormone levels (FSH, LH, and testosterone) and promoted spermatogenesis recovery through their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. The findings demonstrate that corn silk and asparagus officinalis represent promising protective agents against formaldehyde-induced male infertility through modulation of oxidative stress pathways and gene expression regulation.

title: The role of diet and food supplements in infertility management in the Eastern Mediterranean Region: A narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Dietary composition and specific micronutrients significantly influence fertility outcomes in both male and female reproductive function. A Mediterranean-style diet rich in fish, whole grains, vegetables, fruits, and olive oil while low in red meat and processed foods improves semen quality, sperm parameters, and oocyte maturation, whereas red meat consumption reduces sperm motility and anti-Müllerian hormone levels. Antioxidant micronutrients including vitamin C, E, zinc, folate, and β-carotene reduce sperm DNA fragmentation and improve sperm motility and morphology, while vitamin D deficiency correlates with infertility in both sexes, with supplementation restoring vitamin D levels and improving implantation rates and endometrial thickness. Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and their metabolites reduce asthenospermia and inflammatory markers in polycystic ovary syndrome, whereas the eicosapentaenoic acid to arachidonic acid ratio inversely correlates with endometriosis severity. Additionally, myo-inositol and folic acid supplementation enhance mature oocyte production and embryo quality, highlighting the importance of optimized nutritional strategies in managing infertility across the Eastern Mediterranean Region.

title: Oxidative stress as a catalyst in prostate cancer progression: unraveling molecular mechanisms and exploring therapeutic interventions
summary:
# Summary

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) function as a critical catalyst in prostate cancer progression through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Elevated ROS levels directly promote cancer cell proliferation and invasion while simultaneously enhancing angiogenesis, facilitating extracellular matrix remodeling, and activating the unfolded protein response and miRNA expression. Paradoxically, prostate cancer cells develop compensatory antioxidant defenses by upregulating protective enzymes like heme oxygenase-1 and Nrf2, while androgens drive ROS production through the p66Shc-mediated mitochondrial pathway and NADPH oxidase activation. The article reveals that dietary factors (high-fat diet, cholesterol) reduce antioxidant enzyme expression, zinc supplementation and dietary antioxidants (lutein, lycopene) offer protective effects, and androgen deprivation therapy provides clinical benefit by disrupting this ROS-driven progression cascade. Additionally, obesity-associated inflammation creates a feed-forward loop where macrophage-derived cytokines stimulate adipocyte ROS production, ultimately promoting insulin resistance and estrogen-mediated prostate cancer risk.

title: Thalassemia and assisted reproduction: non-transfusion-dependent thalassemia shows no significant effect on live birth rates after embryo transfer
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary: Non-transfusion-dependent thalassemia (NTDT) patients undergoing assisted reproduction show altered ovarian dynamics compared to thalassemia carriers, including reduced oocyte retrieval rates and longer stimulation intervals, yet paradoxically exhibit higher oocyte maturation rates and greater numbers of fertilized oocytes. Iron accumulation in NTDT patients triggers oxidative stress that impairs the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, reducing gonadotropin secretion and subsequently affecting ovarian hormone synthesis and reserve function, while free radicals cause direct oxidative damage to oocyte membranes and DNA. Despite these adverse effects on embryo development metrics, NTDT status shows no statistically significant association with clinical pregnancy rates or live birth rates following embryo transfer, suggesting that compensatory mechanisms or selection during implantation may overcome early developmental disadvantages. Lower AMH and estradiol levels in NTDT patients correlate with fewer punctured follicles and prolonged retrieval duration, while FSH levels demonstrate differential effects on oocyte maturation between groups. These findings indicate that although NTDT-related iron metabolism disruption compromises initial oocyte retrieval and development parameters, it does not substantially impair final reproductive outcomes in the context of modern assisted reproductive technology, potentially due to careful patient selection and the use of preimplantation genetic diagnosis for preventing disease transmission.

title: Ovarian Endometrioma Disrupts Oocyte‐Cumulus Communication and Mitochondrial Function, With Melatonin Mitigating the Effects
summary:
# Summary

Ovarian endometrioma, a severe form of endometriosis, disrupts oocyte-cumulus cell communication and triggers oxidative stress leading to infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The condition impairs transzonal projections and gap junction communication between cumulus cells and oocytes by downregulating critical genes including FSHr, CYP17A1, and CYP19A1, which are essential for steroidogenesis, hormone synthesis, and follicular development. This disruption causes elevated reactive oxygen species, depleted glutathione antioxidant defense, mitochondrial dysfunction with reduced ATP production and membrane potential, DNA damage, and ultimately increased oocyte apoptosis, meiotic arrest, spindle abnormalities, and aneuploidy. Melatonin mitigates these pathological effects by restoring transzonal projections and gap junction functionality, reducing oxidative stress approximately 17-fold, ameliorating DNA damage, enhancing mitochondrial membrane potential, improving meiotic progression rates (germinal vesicle breakdown increased from 87.68% to 97.62%), and significantly reducing aneuploidy and oocyte degeneration. These findings establish oxidative stress and impaired oocyte-cumulus communication as central mechanisms in endometriosis-related infertility and demonstrate melatonin's therapeutic potential as an antioxidant intervention to restore oocyte quality and developmental competence.

title: Exploring melatonin’s multifaceted role in female reproductive health: From follicular development to lactation and its therapeutic potential in obstetric syndromes
summary:
# Summary

Melatonin, primarily synthesized from tryptophan via serotonin and enzymatically processed through arylalkylamine N-acetyltransferase and hydroxyindole-O-methyltransferase, exerts multifaceted protective effects throughout female reproduction. This molecule acts as a potent antioxidant through direct free radical scavenging and indirect upregulation of antioxidant enzymes including superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase, thereby mitigating oxidative stress-induced damage to oocyte quality and reducing reactive oxygen species during follicular development. Melatonin's reproductive benefits are mediated by MT1 and MT2 receptor signaling in diverse tissues—including oocytes, granulosa cells, ovarian luteal cells, uterine myometrium, and hypothalamic-pituitary neurons—promoting oocyte maturation, ATP production, mitochondrial biogenesis, cumulus cell expansion, and progesterone synthesis while inhibiting granulosa cell apoptosis. The hormone also enhances uterine receptivity to facilitate embryo implantation. Abnormal melatonin secretion is associated with endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and preeclampsia, positioning melatonin supplementation as a promising therapeutic strategy for these obstetric syndromes and oxidative stress-related infertility.

title: Genipin attenuates oxidative damage in periodontal tissues by alleviating mitochondrial dysfunction and abnormal glucose uptake through inhibition of UCP2
summary:
# Article Summary

Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction play central roles in periodontitis pathogenesis, with uncoupling protein 2 (UCP2) emerging as a key pathogenic mediator that reduces ATP synthesis and mitochondrial membrane potential while elevating reactive oxygen species production. Genipin, a natural compound with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, attenuates periodontitis by inhibiting UCP2 expression, thereby restoring mitochondrial function and energy metabolism. Through UCP2 inhibition, genipin reduces mitochondrial ROS generation, decreases oxidative stress markers (MDA), enhances antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD), restores ATP synthesis, and upregulates glucose transporter expression (GLUT4) to normalize glucose uptake in periodontal tissues. In both cellular and animal models, genipin treatment suppressed H₂O₂-induced cell apoptosis, improved cell viability, and prevented periodontal tissue destruction including alveolar bone resorption and osteoclast differentiation. These findings demonstrate that genipin protects periodontal tissues from oxidative damage primarily by restoring mitochondrial homeostasis and metabolic efficiency through UCP2 suppression, suggesting a novel therapeutic approach for periodontitis management.

title: Association of composite dietary antioxidant index and endometriosis risk in reproductive—age women: a cross-sectional study using big data-machine learning approach
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress plays a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis by promoting inflammatory responses and abnormal endometrial cell proliferation in reproductive-age women. This cross-sectional study used machine learning approaches to demonstrate that higher composite dietary antioxidant index (CDAI) scores are inversely associated with endometriosis risk, with vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin E, selenium, and carotene identified as key protective components. Endometriosis patients exhibit lower dietary intakes of multiple antioxidants and elevated reactive oxygen species, which drive inflammatory marker production and epithelial-mesenchymal transition through pathways including NF-κB activation. Mechanistically, dietary antioxidants including vitamins C and E, β-carotene, and zinc reduce oxidative damage biomarkers, suppress pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6 and monocyte chemoattractant protein-1, and mitigate endometriosis-associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain. The findings suggest that comprehensive dietary antioxidant intake may serve as a modifiable factor for endometriosis prevention and symptom management in women of reproductive age.

title: Lipid metabolism-associated metabolites on cardiovascular diseases: a two-sample Mendelian randomized study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies lipid metabolism-associated metabolites as critical drivers of cardiovascular disease pathogenesis. Glycolipid metabolism disorders directly cause endothelial dysfunction, inflammation, and atherosclerosis, with specific metabolites showing distinct associations across cardiovascular phenotypes: octadecanedioate and sphingomyelin increase aortic aneurysm risk, while 1-oleoylglycerol and X-21467 elevate atrial fibrillation risk, and tetradecanedioate and hexadecanedioate associate with stroke. The sphingomyelinase-ceramide pathway emerges as a key pro-inflammatory and pro-oxidative mechanism, whereas the sphingosine-1-phosphate signaling axis provides cardioprotection through STAT3 activation and vascular endothelial barrier preservation via S1PR1. N-acetylglutamine, a glutathione precursor involved in antioxidant defense, shows a protective pattern across multiple cardiovascular conditions. These findings suggest that metabolite-level dysregulation in lipid and carbohydrate metabolism, coupled with impaired oxidative stress management, fundamentally drives cardiovascular disease development through both pro-inflammatory and endothelial dysfunction pathways.

title: Mechanisms of Endometrioma-Mediated Ovarian Damage: Myths and Facts
summary:
# Summary: Mechanisms of Endometrioma-Mediated Ovarian Damage

Ovarian endometriomas compromise fertility through multiple interconnected pathways that reduce ovarian reserve and impair follicle development. These lesions directly reduce antral follicle count and anti-Müllerian hormone levels while disrupting folliculogenesis and steroid hormone synthesis, ultimately doubling the risk of infertility. A key mechanism involves oxidative stress within the ovarian microenvironment: iron accumulation in follicular fluid generates reactive oxygen species that damage DNA and accelerate follicular atresia, while elevated free iron triggers local inflammation characterized by heightened IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-8 levels. IL-6 particularly exacerbates the pathology by inhibiting natural killer cells and promoting chronic inflammation, which further impairs oocyte maturation and blastocyst formation. Additionally, dysregulation of signaling pathways including PI3K/AKT/mTOR and elevated extracellular matrix remodeling factors promote fibrosis and granulosa cell apoptosis. Notably, while endometriomas significantly damage ovarian tissue quality and reserve, surprisingly they do not substantially reduce live birth rates in IVF, suggesting that assisted reproductive technologies may partially compensate for the intrinsic ovarian impairment caused by these lesions.

title: Impact of combining laparoscopy with traditional Chinese medicine on oxidative stress in endometriosis-related infertility: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes infertility through a mechanism heavily mediated by oxidative stress, which damages DNA, promotes granulosa cell aging, impairs oocyte function, and compromises endometrial receptivity. Oxidative stress arises from an imbalance between pro-oxidants like reactive oxygen species and antioxidant defenses (superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and vitamin E), while also exacerbating pelvic adhesions and inflammatory responses through increased chemokines such as monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 and RANTES. This meta-analysis demonstrates that combining laparoscopic surgery with traditional Chinese medicine aimed at promoting blood circulation significantly outperforms surgery alone in treating endometriosis-related infertility by substantially reducing oxidative stress markers including reactive oxygen species, 8-isoprostane prostaglandin F2α, and inflammatory chemokines while simultaneously elevating antioxidant enzyme activity and vitamin E levels. The combined approach's superior efficacy in suppressing oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways directly correlates with improved fertility outcomes and reduced endometriotic lesion progression, supporting antioxidant therapy as a critical therapeutic strategy in managing this condition.

title: A case-control study on the role of carbonic anhydrase autoantibodies in the pathogenesis and diagnosis of fibromyalgia
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study identifies elevated autoantibodies against carbonic anhydrase I and II (CAI and CAII) in fibromyalgia syndrome (FMS) patients compared to healthy controls, suggesting an autoimmune component to the disease. Fibromyalgia patients demonstrated significantly elevated oxidative stress markers, including malondialdehyde, total oxidant status, and oxidative stress index, alongside reduced antioxidant capacity, indicating that oxidative stress plays a key pathogenic role in FMS. The study proposes that oxidative modifications inhibit carbonic anhydrase enzyme function, which in turn impairs acid-base balance and CO2 transport—processes essential for normal physiology. These carbonic anhydrase autoantibodies and associated inflammatory responses contribute to tissue damage and peripheral nociceptive activation, particularly through IgG-type autoantibodies that accumulate in dorsal root ganglia to amplify pain sensitivity. The findings establish CAI and CAII autoantibodies as potential diagnostic biomarkers for fibromyalgia and position oxidative stress as a central mechanism linking autoimmunity to the widespread musculoskeletal pain and fatigue characteristic of the syndrome. This research aligns fibromyalgia with other autoimmune and oxidative stress-associated conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematosus.

title: Renal fibrosis progression following partial unilateral ureteral obstruction: mechanisms and therapeutic insights
summary: Partial unilateral ureteral obstruction triggers progressive renal fibrosis through multiple interconnected pathways involving oxidative stress, inflammation, and hemodynamic changes. Mechanical stretching and reduced glomerular filtration rate directly damage tubular epithelial cells and promote apoptosis, while free radicals and reactive oxygen species accumulate due to compromised antioxidant defenses, including reduced catalase and superoxide dismutase levels. Renin-angiotensin system activation serves as a central hub that promotes inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, TGF-β1), downregulates the protective transcription factor Nrf2, and activates pro-fibrotic pathways including Wnt/β-catenin and TGF-β/Smad signaling, which collectively drive extracellular matrix accumulation and fibroblast differentiation. The duration of obstruction is critical—prolonged obstruction leads to irreversible fibrosis and declining kidney function, while short-duration obstruction remains reversible. Notably, endometriosis can cause ureteral obstruction and trigger this cascade. Therapeutic interventions including RAS inhibitors (aliskiren), SGLT-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin), and colchicine show promise by reducing inflammatory markers, suppressing fibrotic pathways, and restoring protective proteins like Klotho, offering potential strategies to prevent progression to end-stage renal disease.

title: Fibrinogen Oxidation and Thrombosis: Shaping Structure and Function
summary:
# Summary

Fibrinogen, a pivotal plasma glycoprotein synthesized by hepatocytes, functions as the precursor to fibrin and plays essential roles beyond hemostasis, including immune responses, inflammation, and tissue repair. Oxidative stress induces fibrinogen oxidation through multiple pathways, with oxidizing agents such as hypochlorite and myeloperoxidase generating over fifty distinct oxidation sites that predominantly affect sulfur-containing amino acids and aromatic residues like tyrosine. These oxidative modifications fundamentally alter fibrin structure, producing denser clots with thinner fibers and reduced permeability while simultaneously decreasing the rate of polymerization and fibrinolysis, changes that are accompanied by reduced α-helical content and altered protein tertiary structure. Oxidized fibrinogen exacerbates prothrombotic conditions by enhancing ADP-induced platelet aggregation, upregulating endothelial adhesion molecules (P-selectin and ICAM-1), and amplifying reactive oxygen species production by leukocytes, thereby perpetuating oxidative stress. Elevated fibrinogen levels and fibrinogen carbonylation—markers of oxidative damage—have been associated with cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and cirrhosis, as well as endometriosis, indicating oxidized fibrinogen as a significant pathogenic factor in multiple disease states involving thrombosis and inflammation.

title: The Oxidative Stress of Human Sperm Cryopreservation
summary:
# Oxidative Stress in Sperm Cryopreservation and Endometriosis-Related Infertility

Cryopreservation of human sperm, while essential for fertility preservation, paradoxically induces oxidative stress through freezing and thawing cycles that increase reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and deplete endogenous antioxidants. This oxidative stress causes cascading damage to sperm, including lipid membrane peroxidation, DNA fragmentation through both single- and double-stranded breaks, and activation of apoptotic pathways via lipid peroxidation byproducts (4-HNE, MDA, acrolein) that disrupt the sperm plasmalemma and impair critical reproductive functions such as capacitation, hyperactivation, and fertilization. The resulting sperm DNA damage is linked to reduced oocyte quality and increased miscarriage risk, while also connecting to elevated oxidative stress observed in endometriosis and other reproductive pathologies. Although ROS at physiological levels are necessary for normal sperm function and oocyte fertilization, excessive oxidative stress from cryopreservation—further amplified by handling procedures like centrifugation and incubation at body temperature—overwhelms cellular defense mechanisms and triggers mitochondrial dysfunction and regulated cell death. The study highlights that maintaining the delicate balance between oxidative and reductive stress is critical for preserving sperm quality during cryopreservation, particularly in contexts of endometriosis-related infertility where baseline ROS elevation already compromises reproductive outcomes.

title: Rubicon, a Key Molecule for Oxidative Stress-Mediated DNA Damage, in Ovarian Granulosa Cells
summary: Aging drives excessive oxidative stress in ovarian granulosa cells, impairing fertility through DNA damage and mitochondrial dysfunction, with this pathology linked to endometriosis, premature ovarian failure, and polycystic ovary syndrome. Rubicon, an autophagy inhibitor, acts as a key mediator of oxidative stress-induced DNA damage in granulosa cells; Rubicon knockdown mitigates this damage while overexpression enhances it, and Rubicon expression is elevated in aged ovaries. Autophagy serves as a protective mechanism against oxidative stress by maintaining cellular homeostasis and promoting granulosa cell survival and differentiation, with autophagy-related genes required for normal reproductive function. The disaccharide trehalose protects granulosa cells against hydrogen peroxide-induced oxidative damage by activating autophagy, downregulating Rubicon expression, and reducing reactive oxygen species production and DNA damage-responsive protein expression. These findings suggest that trehalose may improve ovarian function in patients with age-related infertility and oxidative stress-related diseases by enhancing autophagy and suppressing the Rubicon-mediated pathway of oxidative damage.

title: Methylmercury Chloride Exposure Affects Oocyte Maturation Through AMPK/mTOR-Mediated Mitochondrial Autophagy
summary: Methylmercury chloride (MMC) impairs oocyte maturation through a mechanism centered on excessive mitochondrial autophagy regulated by the AMPK/mTOR pathway. Exposure to MMC triggers multiple detrimental effects including meiotic failure, disrupted cumulus cell expansion, abnormal spindle morphology, and defective chromosome alignment, primarily mediated by mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress. MMC exposure depletes antioxidant defenses (glutathione, superoxide dismutase 2, and catalase), elevates reactive oxygen species and mitochondrial ROS, and damages mitochondrial structure and function, resulting in decreased ATP production and reduced mitochondrial membrane potential. The toxin induces excessive mitochondrial autophagy, which paradoxically worsens oocyte quality by removing functional mitochondria, and this process is triggered by mitochondrial ROS accumulation. MMC also downregulates critical cumulus expansion genes (TNFAIP6, PTGS2, PTX3, and HAS2) while upregulating pro-apoptotic markers (Caspase3 and Bax), leading to cumulus cell apoptosis. These findings demonstrate that MMC's reproductive toxicity, associated with menstrual irregularities, reduced fertilization success, and spontaneous abortion, operates through mitochondrial dysfunction-driven excessive autophagy that can be partially ameliorated by mitochondrial ROS scavengers like Mito-TEMPO.

title: From infection to infertility: a review of the role of human papillomavirus-induced oxidative stress on reproductive health and infertility
summary:
# Summary

Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection induces infertility through multiple pathways centered on oxidative stress (OS) and immune dysregulation. HPV DNA localizes to sperm cells, seminal plasma, and the equatorial region of the sperm head, where it compromises sperm DNA integrity, reduces motility and count, and promotes anti-sperm antibody production that impairs sperm function and cervical mucus penetration. HPV oncoproteins (E1, E2, E6, E7) directly trigger excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) production via NOX2 activation while simultaneously suppressing antioxidant defenses including superoxide dismutase 2, glutathione, and catalase, creating a pathogenic oxidative imbalance that damages mitochondrial function and causes DNA base oxidation. In women, HPV-induced chronic inflammation impairs granulosa cell function and ovarian reserve, while promoting apoptosis in trophoblastic cells through oxidative mechanisms that elevate miscarriage risk. These findings establish HPV-induced oxidative stress as a critical mechanism linking viral infection to reproductive dysfunction in both sexes, with implications for endometriosis and other oxidative stress-associated reproductive disorders.

title: Sperm oxidative damage acquired during seminal plasma removal for assisted reproductive technology is reduced by BGP-15
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

Standard semen preparation procedures for assisted reproductive technology cause oxidative damage to sperm through centrifugation-induced generation of mitochondrial reactive oxygen species, compromising sperm motility, DNA integrity, and ultimately fertilization and embryo outcomes. BGP-15, a mitochondrial-activating compound, significantly mitigates this iatrogenic damage by reducing DNA oxidation by 48-57% and DNA fragmentation by 22% across all preparation methods while simultaneously enhancing sperm motility by 15-17% and mitochondrial membrane potential. Among preparation techniques, swim-up selection produces the highest sperm vitality with the lowest oxidative DNA damage, whereas simple wash yields the highest sperm counts, and density gradient centrifugation demonstrates the highest mitochondrial membrane potential but increased mtROS generation. The incorporation of BGP-15 during semen washing could provide a practical clinical strategy to preserve sperm quality during ART procedures, potentially improving fertilization rates and pregnancy outcomes by protecting against preparation-induced oxidative stress.

title: Association between urinary polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon metabolites and premature menopause: a nationally representative cross-sectional study in the United States
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates a significant association between elevated urinary polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) metabolites—including 1-naphthol, 2-naphthol, 3-fluorene, 2-fluorene, 1-phenanthrene, and 1-pyrene—and premature menopause in a nationally representative U.S. population. PAH metabolites induce premature ovarian aging through multiple interconnected mechanisms: they generate oxidative stress and DNA damage in ovarian granulosa cells and oocytes, trigger follicular atresia and apoptotic signaling, and disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis by activating the aryl hydrocarbon receptor pathway. Additionally, PAHs suppress estradiol synthesis by downregulating aromatase expression through estrogen receptor interactions, thereby impairing follicular growth and ovulation. The study revealed dose-dependent and threshold relationships for several metabolites, with notable racial disparities: 2-naphthol, 3-fluorene, and 2-fluorene showed significant associations only among White participants, while 1-naphthol displayed a protective effect in Black participants, possibly reflecting differences in cytochrome P450 genetic polymorphisms. Beyond reproductive consequences, premature menopause was associated with heightened risks of cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, cognitive decline, and depression, highlighting the broader health implications of PAH exposure.

title: Phenolic-loaded nanofiber from Arctium lappa root: a potential therapy for testosterone-induced ovarian oxidative stress
summary:
# Summary

Testosterone-induced ovarian dysfunction in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) involves excessive oxidative stress and inflammation that impair follicle development and metabolic function. This study demonstrates that nanofiber-encapsulated Arctium lappa phenolic-rich fraction (ALPRF-NF) effectively counters these pathological mechanisms by delivering bioactive phenolic compounds including gallic acid, caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, and naringin that scavenge reactive oxygen species and reduce oxidative damage. ALPRF-NF treatment significantly enhanced the antioxidant defense system by upregulating superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase while simultaneously downregulating the pro-inflammatory enzyme inducible nitric oxide synthase. In a testosterone-induced PCOS mouse model, ALPRF-NF improved metabolic parameters including body weight, feed intake, and liver enzyme profiles while normalizing hormone levels (insulin, luteinizing hormone, estradiol, and follicle-stimulating hormone) and reducing the oxidative stress biomarker malondialdehyde.
