The study identified that a combined dietary nutrient mixture—including copper, vitamin B2, vitamin C, vitamin E, selenium, calcium, beta-carotene, and polyunsaturated fatty acids—was significantly associated with extended reproductive lifespan, while demonstrating non-linear relationships for sodium, iron, and vitamin A. Mechanistically, dietary components influence reproductive lifespan through their effects on oxidative stress and inflammation, both key drivers of follicular atresia and ovarian reserve depletion. Additionally, increased consumption of fruits and protein correlated with extended reproductive lifespan, whereas high-carbohydrate diets were linked to earlier menopause onset. The findings highlight that a truncated reproductive lifespan associates with multiple adverse long-term health outcomes including cardiovascular disease, metabolic dysfunction, osteoporosis, sarcopenia, and neurocognitive decline, underscoring the importance of nutritional factors as modifiable regulators of reproductive aging and overall health.

title: Mechanisms and treatment modalities related to premature ovarian insufficiency in mitochondria: literature review
summary:
# Mitochondrial Dysfunction and Oxidative Stress in Premature Ovarian Insufficiency

Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is strongly associated with mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress, which together drive accelerated oocyte apoptosis, granulosa cell dysfunction, and premature follicle loss. Oxidative stress promotes mitochondrial dysfunction through excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, creating a self-perpetuating cycle where dysfunctional mitochondria further exacerbate ROS generation and compromise oocyte quality. Multiple therapeutic approaches target this pathology: natural compounds like theophylline derivatives and Cuscuta species extracts activate the PI3K/Akt and Keap1-Nrf2-ARE antioxidant pathways to reduce ROS and promote cell survival; melatonin restores critical mitochondrial dynamics proteins (FIS1 and MFN1) to improve ovarian function; and coenzyme Q10 upregulates the SIRT1/PGC-1α axis to enhance mitochondrial biogenesis and function. Emerging therapies including mesenchymal stem cell-derived mitochondria, human umbilical cord stem cells, acupuncture, and amniotic fluid-derived exosomes all converge on restoring mitochondrial biogenesis and activating antioxidant defense mechanisms. Enhanced mtDNA copy number correlates with improved oocyte and granulosa cell quality, suggesting that restoring mitochondrial health through these interventions may reverse the mitochondrial-oxidative stress pathology underlying POI-related infertility.

title: The omega-3 DHA induces pyroptosis and mitochondrial dysfunction in ovarian cancer cells via ROS and caspase-1 activation
summary:
# Summary

Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA), an omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid, induces pyroptosis in ovarian cancer cells through a ROS-dependent mechanism centered on caspase-1 activation and mitochondrial dysfunction. DHA treatment rapidly increases intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) and mitochondrial superoxide levels, which are essential for triggering membrane pore formation, loss of mitochondrial membrane potential, and GSDMD cleavage—hallmarks of pyroptosis. The ROS-dependent nature of DHA's effects is demonstrated by the fact that N-acetylcysteine, a ROS scavenger, completely prevents both pyroptosis and mitochondrial damage, while caspase-1 inhibition restores mitochondrial integrity and respiratory function. Additionally, DHA reduces mitochondrial content and spare respiratory capacity in A2780 ovarian cancer cells, demonstrating that caspase-1 activation is a key mediator of DHA-induced bioenergetic dysfunction. These findings establish DHA as a promising anti-cancer therapeutic agent with multi-faceted mechanisms of action against ovarian cancer cells, potentially relevant to endometriosis-associated malignancy prevention given the known link between endometriosis and ovarian cancer risk.

title: Effect of type 2 diabetes mellitus on sperm quality and outcomes of assisted reproductive techniques in infertile male patients
summary: Type 2 diabetes mellitus significantly impairs male fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving oxidative stress, hormonal disruption, and vascular complications. Hyperglycemia elevates reactive oxygen species production, which increases sperm DNA fragmentation and reduces sperm motility, while accumulated advanced glycation end-products further impair sperm function by decreasing motility and increasing deformity rates. Additionally, hyperglycemia causes mitochondrial dysfunction leading to insufficient ATP production in sperm, and disrupts glucose metabolism critical for spermatogenesis. The disease also suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis by altering GnRH pulse release and subsequently reducing LH and FSH production, impairing seminiferous tubule function and testosterone synthesis. Diabetic complications including microvascular and neuropathic damage cause erectile and ejaculatory dysfunction, reducing semen volume and emission. These combined effects—reduced sperm volume and motility, elevated DNA fragmentation, and hormonal insufficiency—collectively result in decreased fertilization rates, embryonic developmental arrest, and increased miscarriage risk, necessitating assisted reproductive techniques such as in vitro fertilization and intracytoplasmic sperm injection for diabetic men seeking conception.

title: Excessive Vitamin E Supplementation: Implications for Ovarian Physiology and Reproductive Health
summary: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play essential roles in ovarian physiology, functioning as secondary messengers in critical processes including meiosis, ovulation, folliculogenesis, and oocyte maturation, while disturbances in redox balance contribute to female infertility and conditions like endometriosis and polycystic ovarian syndrome. Although moderate vitamin E supplementation provides beneficial antioxidant effects by protecting plasma membranes and enhancing reproductive parameters such as cervical mucus production, sperm viability, and endometrial thickness, excessive vitamin E intake paradoxically disrupts the delicate redox equilibrium in ovarian tissues. Vitamin E overdose increases oxidative stress markers (elevated ROS and GSH/GSSG ratios) in granulosa cells while failing to activate compensatory antioxidant defenses via the Nrf2 pathway, ultimately impairing folliculogenesis and reducing antral follicles, corpora lutea, and viable oocyte numbers. The excessive supplementation also significantly reduces pregnancy rates, litter size, blastocyst formation rates, and embryo quality while decreasing uterine thickness and GV oocyte recovery. Additionally, vitamin E overdose suppresses apoptosis through reduced Bax/Bcl2 ratios, potentially allowing accumulation of abnormal oocytes and follicles, thereby demonstrating that supraphysiological antioxidant levels can be detrimental to reproductive outcomes.

title: Strategies for overcoming multiple barriers of oral administration of protein and peptide therapeutics
summary:
# Summary

Oral delivery of protein and peptide therapeutics offers a patient-friendly alternative to parenteral administration, but multiple gastrointestinal barriers severely limit their absorption and efficacy. The harsh GI environment poses interconnected challenges including extreme pH conditions that denature protein structures and promote oxidative degradation, diverse proteolytic enzymes (pepsin, trypsin, chymotrypsin, elastase, and carboxypeptidases) that cleave peptide bonds, and reducing agents like glutathione that disrupt critical disulfide linkages. Additional physical barriers—including the mucus layer that traps proteins, tight junctions that restrict paracellular transport, and the hydrophilic nature of these therapeutics that prevents transcellular absorption—further compromise oral bioavailability. Current successful strategies include pH modulation using buffering agents like SNAC (used in oral semaglutide), enteric coating to protect therapeutics during gastric transit, chemical modifications such as N-terminal acetylation to enhance pH stability, and nanoparticle conjugation (exemplified by silver sulfide quantum dot-insulin formulations) to shield therapeutic molecules from degradation. These multifaceted approaches demonstrate that overcoming oral delivery barriers requires integrated solutions addressing chemical stability, enzymatic protection, and membrane transport simultaneously.

title: NMN supplementation as a strategy to improve oocyte quality: a systematic review and transcriptomic analysis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines nicotinamide mononucleotide (NMN) supplementation as a strategy to enhance oocyte quality by restoring NAD⁺ levels and mitochondrial function. NAD⁺ is a critical coenzyme regulating mitochondrial function, oxidative stress response, and DNA repair through NAD⁺-dependent enzymes including sirtuins; however, NAD⁺ levels decline with age and metabolic stress, impairing oocyte quality. Transcriptomic analysis identified differential gene expression patterns across oocyte developmental stages, with mitochondrial regulators (SIRT3, DNM1L, SOD1) upregulated in germinal vesicle oocytes, while genes associated with apoptosis and inflammation were downregulated. NMN supplementation restores intracellular NAD⁺ levels and activates sirtuin-dependent pathways (particularly SIRT1 and SIRT3), thereby improving mitochondrial regulation, reducing oxidative stress, and modulating inflammatory and apoptotic pathways. In metabolically stressed models (high-fat diet and type 1 diabetes exposure), mitochondrial dysfunction is accompanied by downregulation of key regulatory proteins and antioxidant enzymes, changes that NMN supplementation can partially reverse. These findings suggest NMN represents a promising intervention to counteract age- and metabolic stress-related oocyte quality decline through NAD⁺-dependent mitochondrial restoration.

title: SIRT3 attenuates AGEs-induced senescence in human granulosa cells through enhancing mitophagy
summary: Advanced glycation end products (AGEs) impair human granulosa cell function and fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms, inducing premature senescence by suppressing mitophagy, impairing mitochondrial function, and reducing steroid hormone production. AGEs accumulate in the follicular microenvironment and trigger senescence through oxidative stress, enhanced ROS production, DNA damage, and activation of apoptotic pathways involving p53, p21, and caspase-3. The mitochondrial protein deacetylase SIRT3 emerges as a critical protective factor that attenuates AGEs-induced senescence by enhancing PINK1-Parkin-mediated mitophagy, restoring mitochondrial homeostasis, ATP production, and crucially, promoting synthesis of estradiol-17β and progesterone. Activation of mitophagy through urolithin A reverses AGEs-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and senescence, whereas blocking mitophagy with cyclosporine A exacerbates these effects, establishing mitophagy as a key therapeutic target. These findings illuminate how oxidative stress-induced mitophagy deficiency drives endometriosis-related infertility via granulosa cell senescence and suggest SIRT3 activation and mitophagy enhancement as potential interventions to preserve ovarian function and reproductive capacity.

title: Metabolism-Regulating Microspheres: Design Principles, Therapeutic Applications Across Multisystem Diseases, and Future Perspectives
summary:
# Summary

Metabolic dysregulation drives the pathogenesis of multiple chronic diseases through interconnected mechanisms including glycolytic dysregulation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and oxidative stress. Metabolism-regulating microspheres represent a versatile therapeutic platform targeting these metabolic abnormalities across diverse conditions, including orthopedic diseases (osteoporosis, osteoarthritis, bone defects), ophthalmic disorders (glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy), and gynecological diseases (endometriosis, premature ovarian insufficiency, ovarian cancer). In osteoarthritis, dysregulated glycolytic flux and aberrant lipid metabolism drive lactic acidosis and chondrocyte inflammation leading to cartilage destruction, which microspheres address by delivering therapeutic ions (Mg²⁺, Ce³⁺/Ce⁴⁺, Zn²⁺) and metabolites (α-ketoglutarate, NAD⁺, l-glutamine) that restore metabolic homeostasis and suppress inflammatory responses. For endometriosis, microspheres reduce estradiol levels and downregulate progesterone receptor signaling to treat disease progression. Advanced microsphere designs employ stimuli-responsive release mechanisms, targeted mitochondrial delivery, and programmable ion kinetics to optimize therapeutic efficacy and tissue-specific metabolic reprogramming.

title: Reactive Oxygen Species in Embryo Development: Sources, Impacts, and Implications for In Vitro Culture Systems
summary: While physiological concentrations of reactive oxygen species (ROS) are essential for normal fertilization and early embryonic development—regulating sperm capacitation, oocyte activation, pronuclear formation, and blastocyst maturation—excessive ROS production overwhelms antioxidant defenses and causes oxidative stress. Elevated ROS impairs multiple developmental processes by inducing DNA fragmentation, lipid peroxidation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and apoptosis, ultimately disrupting oocyte fertilization, embryonic cleavage, and blastocyst formation. In vitro culture systems are particularly vulnerable to ROS accumulation due to suboptimal laboratory conditions including high oxygen tension, temperature fluctuations, and light exposure, which generate ROS from multiple sources such as oxidative phosphorylation, NADPH oxidase, and xanthine oxidase. ROS accumulation specifically disrupts mitochondrial structure and function by reducing membrane potential and interfering with ATP synthesis, while even mild oxidative stress triggers cell cycle arrest and programmed cell death. These findings highlight that in vitro embryo culture conditions can generate significantly more ROS than the natural in vivo environment, compromising fertilization rates and preimplantation blastocyst quality in assisted reproductive technologies. Understanding ROS regulation is therefore critical for optimizing in vitro culture systems and improving outcomes in reproductive medicine, particularly in conditions associated with oxidative stress such as endometriosis-related infertility.

title: A Review of Traditional Chinese Medicine Formulations and Natural Active Ingredients with Therapeutic Potential for Male Infertility Targeting Oxidative Stress
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress represents a critical mechanism underlying male infertility, causing sperm structural and functional abnormalities through excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation that disrupts redox homeostasis, damages sperm membranes, and compromises nuclear DNA integrity. Multiple exogenous factors including drugs (cyclophosphamide, triptolide), environmental pollutants (bisphenol A, phthalates, heavy metals), and preservatives trigger pathological ROS accumulation in sperm, while physiological ROS concentrations normally regulate essential sperm functions like capacitation and the acrosome reaction. Sperm possess limited antioxidant defenses via superoxide dismutase and glutathione peroxidase, and their polyunsaturated fatty acid-rich membranes render them particularly vulnerable to oxidative damage. Traditional Chinese medicine formulations including the Wuzi Yanzong Prescription and Bazi Bushen Prescription demonstrate therapeutic potential by harnessing bioactive constituents—polyphenols, flavonoids, and polysaccharides—that scavenge ROS and enhance antioxidant enzyme activity. In vivo and in vitro studies confirm these formulations improve sperm parameters, testicular function, testosterone levels, and reduce oxidative markers like malondialdehyde while suppressing apoptotic pathways in testicular cells. These findings support the therapeutic application of natural antioxidant compounds from Traditional Chinese Medicine for addressing oxidative stress-induced male infertility.

title: Clinical rationale behind glucose-sparing strategies in peritoneal dialysis: a narrative review
summary:
# Summary

Glucose-based peritoneal dialysis solutions cause significant peritoneal membrane damage through multiple mechanisms, including oxidative stress, fibrosis, and impaired ultrafiltration, while also triggering systemic metabolic complications such as hyperglycemia, obesity, and cardiovascular disease. To mitigate these glucose-induced injuries, glucose-sparing strategies employ alternative osmotic agents like icodextrin and xylitol, which preserve residual kidney function and reduce fibrosis without glucose toxicity. Metabolic interventions targeting glycolytic pathways—including pyruvate activation, carnitine supplementation, and glycolytic inhibitors like 2-deoxyglucose—reduce transforming growth factor-beta-driven fibrotic phenotypes and extracellular matrix damage. Antioxidant therapies such as N-acetylcysteine, pyruvate, and alanyl-glutamine-supplemented dialysate counteract reactive oxygen species and restore mesothelial integrity. Emerging approaches including SGLT2 inhibitors, incremental dialysis regimens, and neutral-pH low-GDP solutions further preserve peritoneal membrane structure and vascular function while reducing peritonitis and sclerosing peritonitis risk. Collectively, these glucose-sparing strategies represent a paradigm shift toward biocompatible dialysis solutions that maintain clinical efficacy while substantially reducing long-term peritoneal membrane injury and patient morbidity.

title: Preventive Role of Simvastatin against Infertility: Potential Role of PGC-α/PPARβ/UCPs and NF-κβ Signaling Pathways
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction form a central pathogenic axis in infertility-related conditions, with oxidative stress triggering mitochondrial dysfunction that subsequently initiates apoptosis, inflammation, and germ cell degeneration through reactive oxygen species (ROS) production and lipid peroxidation. Inactivation of key antioxidant enzymes (GPx, GR, SOD, CAT) disrupts germ cell function, while dysregulated inflammatory signaling—involving elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), reduced anti-inflammatory agents (IL-2, IL-4, IL-10), and overactivation of NF-κβ and COX-PG pathways—exacerbates infertility in both males and females. The PGC-α/PPARβ/UCPs mitochondrial regulatory pathway represents a critical protective mechanism that can suppress NF-κβ signaling and thereby inhibit oxidative stress, inflammation, and apoptosis. Simvastatin emerges as a preventive therapeutic agent that inhibits oxidative stress through antioxidant and cell-protective properties, offering potential as a novel intervention to restore mitochondrial function and reduce infertility risk associated with chronic metabolic diseases such as diabetes and obesity. These findings highlight a multi-level pathogenic cascade where mitochondrial health and oxidative-inflammatory balance are fundamental to maintaining germ cell viability and fertility.

title: Erzhi Tiangui formula increases the number of high-quality blastocysts in patients with advanced age by regulating the Nrf2/HO-1 signaling pathway: a randomized controlled study
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled study demonstrates that the Erzhi Tiangui (EZTG) formula improves embryo quality in advanced-age patients undergoing in vitro fertilization by activating the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant signaling pathway. The EZTG formula reduced oxidative stress and granulosa cell apoptosis by activating Nrf2, which translocates to the nucleus, binds antioxidant response elements, and induces expression of HO-1 and protective antioxidant enzymes including SOD, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase. In the EZTG-treated group, significantly elevated levels of glutathione and ATP were observed alongside upregulation of anti-apoptotic proteins (Bcl-2) and downregulation of pro-apoptotic markers (Bax, Caspase-3, Keap1). Clinically, patients receiving the EZTG formula produced significantly higher numbers of high-quality blastocysts and improved fertilization rates compared to controls. These findings establish that the EZTG formula mitigates oxidative stress-induced oocyte deterioration through Nrf2/HO-1 pathway activation, providing a mechanistic basis for herbal formula application in improving IVF outcomes for older women with compromised ovarian function.

title: Mycotoxin-Induced Oxidative Stress and Its Impact on Human Folliculogenesis: Examining the Link to Reproductive Health
summary:
# Summary

This study examines how environmental mycotoxins compromise human ovarian function through oxidative stress pathways relevant to reproductive health. Multiple mycotoxins—including deoxynivalenol (DON), zearalenone (ZEN) and its metabolite alpha-ZOL, and aflatoxin M1—penetrate follicular fluid and trigger oxidative stress by disrupting mitochondrial function, elevating the lipid peroxidation marker malondialdehyde (MDA) and impairing antioxidant enzyme activity. The research reveals complex endocrine disruption mechanisms, particularly that zearalenone acts as an estrogen-like compound to impair ovarian function, while alpha-ZOL strongly correlates with elevated cortisol levels that suppress protective antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, GPx) and estradiol production—effects modulated by follicular estrogen availability. Estradiol and progesterone emerge as protective factors that upregulate antioxidant defenses and total antioxidant capacity (TAOC), while DON paradoxically triggers compensatory SOD increases yet simultaneously disrupts follicle development through interactions with anti-Müllerian hormone signaling. These findings establish mycotoxin-induced oxidative stress as a mechanistic link between environmental toxin exposure and folliculogenesis impairment, with potential relevance to understanding reproductive dysfunction including endometriosis-related infertility.

title: New alternatives in photoprotection: Preparation and evaluation of lamellar silicate derivatives and their use as sunscreens
summary:
# Summary: Lamellar Silicate Derivatives as Advanced Photoprotective Agents

UV radiation from both UVA and UVB wavelengths causes DNA damage through multiple mechanisms, including cyclobutane pyrimidine dimer formation and reactive oxygen species-mediated single-strand breaks, ultimately leading to photoaging and photocarcinogenesis. This study evaluated lamellar silicate nanostructures incorporating organic sunscreen filters (DHHB, OMC, and BEMT) as alternatives to conventional photoprotection, with the composite formulation S4+OMC-NC demonstrating enhanced cell survival and antimutagenic effects when exposed to simulated solar light. Individual filters showed variable efficacy: VS4 and TiO₂-doped structures were photoprotective and prevented mutation, while BEMT paradoxically increased mutation frequency through excessive reactive oxygen species generation. The intercalation of OMC into organophilic clay surfaces reduced transdermal absorption and prevented free radical formation, and the nanometric size of these systems increased surface contact area for more efficient radiation absorption. These nanotechnology-based formulations represent a promising advance in photoprotection, combining the protective mechanisms of UV-absorbing filters with the safety benefits of clay nanostructures while minimizing potential mutagenic effects of certain organic filters.

title: Dietary β-carotene improves the ovary development and antioxidant capacity of replacement gilts
journal: Journal of Animal Science and Biotechnology
summary:
# Summary

Dietary β-carotene enhances ovarian development and reproductive capacity in replacement gilts through two interconnected mechanisms: promoting lipolysis and activating antioxidant defenses. β-Carotene directly upregulates the transcription factor FOXL2 in granulosa cells, which then binds to the StAR promoter and increases expression of steroidogenic enzymes (StAR and CYP11A1), ultimately elevating estradiol synthesis in follicular fluid and blood. Simultaneously, β-carotene promotes lipid mobilization by downregulating the lipid droplet protein PLIN2 while upregulating lipolytic enzyme LIPE and fatty acid oxidation enzyme ACSL4, providing substrate and energy (ATP) for hormone synthesis and increasing medium-sized follicles (2–5 mm diameter). Additionally, β-carotene increases antioxidant enzyme activity of SOD1 and GPX4 in both serum and follicular fluid, effectively reducing reactive oxygen species and maintaining redox balance in ovarian tissues. These effects appear to be specific to β-carotene itself rather than its metabolite retinoic acid, as retinoic acid did not upregulate FOXL2, estradiol, or key lipolytic enzymes, highlighting a ligand-specific signaling mechanism independent of vitamin A metabolism.

title: Differential cytotoxicity mechanisms of copper complexed with disulfiram for combination chemotherapy in human endometrial cancer cells
summary:
# Summary

Disulfiram (DSF) complexed with copper ions represents a promising combination chemotherapy approach for endometrial cancer, operating through multiple oxidative stress mechanisms. Both DSF/Cu+ and DSF/Cu2+ complexes potently induce apoptosis, autophagy, and DNA damage in endometrial cancer cells by generating cytosolic and mitochondrial reactive oxygen species and triggering endoplasmic reticulum stress, with the Cu+ complex uniquely causing mitochondrial depolarization. The anticancer activity stems from DSF's conversion to diethyldithiocarbamate, which chelates copper to act as an ionophore that elevates intracellular copper and catalyzes the Fenton reaction, producing reactive hydroxyl radicals that overwhelm cellular antioxidant capacity. Additionally, DSF suppresses multiple oncogenic pathways including ROS, PI3K, MAPK, and NF-κB signaling while targeting proteostasis through disruption of the p97/VCP segregase pathway. The DSF/Cu+ complex synergizes specifically with doxorubicin in RL95-2 cells, whereas DSF, CuCl, and CuCl2 broadly synergize with both cisplatin and doxorubicin across endometrial cancer cell lines, offering a multifaceted strategy to overcome chemoresistance in this highly prevalent gynecologic malignancy.

title: Energy Allocation Resilience and Endocrine Integration
summary:
# Summary

This article presents a comprehensive model of energy allocation resilience in endometriosis-related infertility, proposing that mitochondrial reserve capacity functions as a system-wide constraint on adaptive stress responses. Low-grade chronic inflammation and sustained hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activation impair mitochondrial function by reducing oxidative phosphorylation efficiency and diverting ATP away from reproductive and endocrine signaling, while pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and TNF-α) directly increase oxidative stress. The hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid (HPT) axis modulates metabolic pace through thyroid hormones that enhance mitochondrial respiration, but stress-induced elevation of reverse triiodothyronine reduces thyroid hormone bioavailability and further compromises mitochondrial efficiency. Multiple modifiable factors—including micronutrient deficiency (iron, selenium, iodine, folate, vitamins D and B12), physical inactivity, circadian disruption, and insulin resistance—constrain bioenergetic reserves, whereas physical activity and adequate micronutrition enhance mitochondrial biogenesis and stress adaptation. The model explains how preserved mitochondrial function enables tolerance of repeated stressors with minimal functional decline, while compromised energy metabolism manifests as fatigue, cognitive impairment, and reproductive dysfunction characteristic of endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Fatty Acid Amide Hydrolase Signaling and Ovarian Disorders: From Molecular Mechanism to Clinical Significance
summary: Fatty acid amide hydrolase (FAAH) is a central regulator of ovarian physiology that controls intracellular levels of the endocannabinoid anandamide (AEA) in granulosa cells, oocytes, and luteal cells, thereby modulating follicular development, steroidogenesis, oocyte maturation, and luteal function. FAAH is highly expressed in dominant follicles where it maintains low AEA levels to support granulosa cell proliferation and competence, while reduced FAAH expression in atretic follicles allows AEA accumulation that triggers CB1-mediated apoptosis. During the luteal phase, FAAH expression increases during luteinization to facilitate progesterone synthesis by suppressing AEA, while declining during luteal regression to permit apoptosis. Altered FAAH activity—including insufficient activity from reduced expression or pharmacological inhibition—leads to pathological AEA accumulation that impairs follicular growth, oocyte quality, aromatase-mediated estrogen synthesis, and ovulation, contributing to polycystic ovary syndrome, premature ovarian insufficiency, and infertility. FAAH expression is dynamically regulated by steroid hormones (estrogen and progesterone), inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6), and oocyte-derived growth factors (GDF9 and BMP15), enabling integration of multiple ovarian signaling pathways. These findings establish FAAH as a critical molecular hub linking endocannabinoid signaling to reproductive hormone metabolism and follicular selection, with therapeutic implications for treating endometriosis-associated infertility and other ovarian disorders.

title: Metabolomic profiling of follicular fluid reveals unique pathways in endometriosis and infertility etiologies: a pilot study
summary:
# Summary

Metabolomic profiling of follicular fluid reveals distinct metabolic disruptions in endometriosis patients that impair reproductive outcomes. Endometriosis is characterized by reduced steroid biosynthesis and elevated thiamine metabolism, alongside increased oxidative stress markers including 3-chloro-L-tyrosine and 8-oxoerythraline, which negatively correlate with follicle development rate and high-quality embryo rate. Key metabolites such as D-mannosamine and D-galacturonic acid are reduced in endometriosis but positively associate with follicle development, while pyruvate and lactate support oocyte quality and fertilization. The dysregulated metabolic pathways in endometriosis involve impaired steroid hormone biosynthesis and enhanced thiamine metabolism, contrasting with distinct patterns observed in polycystic ovary syndrome and other infertility etiologies. A predictive biomarker model identified D-mannosamine, D-galacturonic acid, and 3-chloro-L-tyrosine as potential endometriosis biomarkers, suggesting that targeted metabolomic analysis of follicular fluid can distinguish endometriosis from other causes of female infertility and inform mechanistic understanding of endometriosis-related reproductive dysfunction.

title: Unveiling the anti-glycation properties of acetylsalicylic acid: targeted inhibition of albumin glycation
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that acetylsalicylic acid (aspirin) exhibits potent anti-glycation properties by inhibiting the modification of human serum albumin (HSA), a critical blood protein responsible for transporting biomolecules and maintaining antioxidant activity. Glycation—a non-enzymatic process where reducing sugars bind to proteins—progressively forms Advanced Glycation End Products (AGEs) and induces oxidative stress, ultimately impairing HSA's structural and functional integrity while promoting protein aggregation and reducing antioxidant capacity. Aspirin counteracts these glycation-induced modifications through multiple mechanisms: acetylating specific lysine residues on HSA, restoring free thiol and amino groups, reducing glycation byproducts (pentosidine, malondialdehyde, fructosamine), and decreasing β-amyloid aggregate formation and protein turbidity. These protective effects restore HSA's hydrophobicity index, increase its antioxidant power, and reduce oxidative protein damage markers, thereby mitigating the cascade of glycation-related complications including neuropathy, nephropathy, and atherosclerosis. The findings establish aspirin as a promising therapeutic agent for preventing protein glycation-induced pathology in hyperglycemic states.

title: Age-related mitochondrial energy metabolism reprogramming occurs in granulosa cells during ovarian aging
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that granulosa cells undergo significant mitochondrial energy metabolism reprogramming during ovarian aging, characterized by a pathological shift from oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) to glycolysis. Advanced maternal age (AMA) granulosa cells show upregulated glycolytic metabolites while exhibiting downregulated OXPHOS and tricarboxylic acid cycle intermediates, supporting oocytes with altered nutrient profiles that compromise developmental potential. In vitro oxidative stress models using hydrogen peroxide confirmed that oxidative damage triggers mitochondrial dysfunction and cellular senescence in granulosa cells, accompanied by increased lactate production, reduced oxygen consumption, and elevated mitochondrial reactive oxygen species. These age-associated glycometabolic perturbations represent a novel mechanism linking mitochondrial dysfunction to impaired fertility in women with advanced maternal age, suggesting that metabolic reprogramming may be a therapeutic target for addressing age-related reproductive decline and infertility.

title: Protective effect of a novel hydrogel loaded with CM-UCMSCs on vitrified–thawed ovaries during in vitro culture
summary: This study demonstrates that a novel antioxidant hydrogel (PG-gel) loaded with conditioned medium from umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (CM-UCMSCs) provides significant protection to vitrified-thawed ovarian tissue during in vitro culture. The PG-gel, composed of N-acetylcysteine-modified gelatin crosslinked with PEG-SS, suppresses intracellular reactive oxygen species generation through both direct ROS scavenging by sulfhydryl groups and release of bioactive factors including exosomes. When combined with CM-UCMSCs, this system markedly reduced follicle loss, collagen deposition, and apoptosis while enhancing glucose consumption and energy metabolism in cultured ovarian tissue. Transcriptomic analysis revealed that PG-gel with CM-UCMSCs downregulated apoptosis-related genes (Ddit3, Trib3, Slc1a4, Sqstm1) and simultaneously upregulated mitochondrial metabolism genes (Mt-atp8, Mt-nd1, Mt-nd2, Mt-nd4, Mt-cyb), indicating improved mitochondrial function and metabolic recovery. These findings suggest that this hydrogel-based approach effectively mitigates ischemia-reperfusion injury and oxidative stress in ovarian grafts, preserving ovarian reserve and follicle quality during transplantation procedures.

title: A prospective pilot study on DACHAO intervention for improving embryo quality metrics in IVF/ICSI failure cases
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

DACHAO Reco18, a traditional Chinese herbal formulation, significantly improves embryo quality and fertility outcomes in IVF/ICSI patients with prior cycle failures through multiple complementary mechanisms. The intervention increased oocyte yield, fertilization rates, and high-quality embryo production by over 118%, while substantially improving implantation (0–32%), biochemical pregnancy (0–24%), and clinical pregnancy (0–21%) rates, alongside reducing FSH levels in hypergonadotropic patients. DACHAO achieves these benefits by reducing oxidative stress and inflammation, enhancing mitochondrial membrane potential, and suppressing pro-apoptotic pathways (downregulating cleaved caspase-3 and PARP), while elevating serum estradiol and promoting granulosa cell proliferation. The formulation contains multiple bioactive compounds—including quercetin, eugenol, and inositol—that synergistically combat the oxidative damage, chromosomal abnormalities, and mitochondrial dysfunction that underlie poor embryo development in advanced maternal age and repeated IVF failures. These findings suggest DACHAO represents a promising adjunctive approach for improving embryo quality and clinical pregnancy outcomes in poor responders and patients with endometriosis-related infertility.

title: Boosting Cuproptosis in Breast Cancer Therapy via Photodynamic Treatment With a New Liposome
summary: This study develops a novel liposomal nanoparticle formulation (ICG@ES-Cu Lip) combining photodynamic therapy (PDT) with cuproptosis induction for breast cancer treatment. The liposome delivers indocyanine green (ICG), a near-infrared photosensitizer, alongside elesclomol-complexed copper ions that trigger cuproptosis—a copper-dependent form of cell death involving mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress. Near-infrared laser activation of ICG generates reactive oxygen species that synergistically enhance copper ion-induced cuproptosis by depleting cellular glutathione antioxidant reserves and elevating lipid peroxidation markers (malondialdehyde). In MCF-7 breast cancer cells, combined ICG@ES-Cu Lip plus near-infrared treatment significantly downregulated cuproptosis-regulatory genes (DLAT, LIAS, FDX1, NLP4), reduced mitochondrial membrane potential, and induced extensive apoptosis and tumor necrosis with superior efficacy compared to single-modality treatments. The liposomal delivery platform enhanced drug stability, tumor targeting, and bioavailability while maintaining good biosafety in vivo. This combination therapy overcomes the individual limitations of PDT alone in the hypoxic, antioxidant-rich tumor microenvironment by exploiting complementary copper-induced and ROS-mediated cytotoxic pathways.

title: Dysregulated post-translational modifications in granulosa cells drive ovarian dysfunction and potential infertility applications (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Granulosa cells orchestrate follicular development and ovarian function through tightly regulated post-translational modifications (PTMs) that control cell proliferation, differentiation, apoptosis, and hormone secretion. Dysregulation of these PTMs—including aberrant phosphorylation, histone methylation patterns, and disrupted signaling cascades—contributes significantly to female infertility by impairing granulosa cell function and compromising oocyte quality. Oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and endoplasmic reticulum stress emerge as key mechanisms driving granulosa cell dysfunction, leading to primordial follicle depletion, disrupted folliculogenesis, and ovulation disorders. Specific histone modifications (H3K4me3, H3K9me3, H3K27me3) act as epigenetic switches regulating progesterone production and estrogen synthesis, with dysregulation contributing to endometriosis and reproductive dysfunction. Therapeutic interventions including melatonin supplementation, myo-inositol, electro-acupuncture, and targeted inhibitors like rapamycin and ISRIB show promise in restoring granulosa cell function and improving ovarian physiology. Understanding these PTM-dependent regulatory mechanisms provides mechanistic insights into endometriosis-related infertility and identifies novel therapeutic targets for fertility preservation and assisted reproductive technology outcomes.

title: Association of metal exposure in follicular fluid with assisted reproductive technology outcomes in East Chinese women
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how metal exposure in follicular fluid affects assisted reproductive technology (ART) outcomes in Chinese women undergoing fertility treatment. Higher levels of zinc and iron in follicular fluid were positively associated with high-quality embryo production, while elevated calcium and copper levels correlated with reduced oocyte maturation rates. Lead exposure significantly impaired reproductive outcomes by reducing high-quality embryo rates and clinical pregnancy success through multiple mechanisms including oxidative stress induction, cell apoptosis, and suppression of hormones like anti-Müllerian hormone and estradiol. Heavy metals including lead and cadmium exert reproductive toxicity by disrupting the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and interfering with hormone synthesis. These findings underscore the critical balance of essential trace metals—zinc, iron, manganese, calcium, magnesium, and copper—which function as enzymatic cofactors required for normal reproductive function and oocyte development, while toxic metal accumulation substantially compromises fertility outcomes.

title: Telmisartan protects male reproductive function by modulating biochemical, oxidative stress, apoptosis, and DNA integrity in cyclophosphamide-exposed rats
summary: Cyclophosphamide causes severe testicular injury through multiple pathways: its active metabolite phosphoramide mustard induces DNA damage and cross-linking, while acrolein generates excessive reactive oxygen species that deplete antioxidant defenses (glutathione, catalase, superoxide dismutase), leading to oxidative stress that disrupts the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and triggers germ cell apoptosis and hypospermatogenesis. This oxidative stress also suppresses testosterone, luteinizing hormone, and follicle-stimulating hormone while promoting apoptosis through elevated Bax and caspase-3 and reduced Bcl-2, ultimately causing infertility. Telmisartan protects testicular function through dual mechanisms: blocking angiotensin II type 1 receptor to prevent the pro-inflammatory and pro-apoptotic effects of angiotensin II, and activating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma and the Nrf2 antioxidant pathway. Nrf2 activation restores antioxidant enzyme expression (heme oxygenase-1, NAD(P)H quinone oxidoreductase-1) and reduces oxidative stress, while simultaneously suppressing pro-apoptotic markers and upregulating anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 in a dose-dependent manner. This comprehensive protection by telmisartan restores hormone levels, improves sperm quality, and preserves DNA integrity in cyclophosphamide-exposed rats, demonstrating potential clinical utility for protecting male reproductive function during chemotherapy.

title: Areca Nut Chewing as a Modifiable Risk Factor for Maternal Anemia, Prematurity and Fetal Low Birth Weight: A Cross Sectional Study in Teluk Wondama, West Papua
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study demonstrates that areca nut chewing during pregnancy is a strong modifiable risk factor for maternal anemia, prematurity, and low birth weight, with affected mothers showing 11.4-fold increased odds of anemia and over 10-fold increased risk of preterm delivery. The adverse effects operate through multiple interconnected mechanisms: the alkaloid arecoline induces oxidative stress and suppresses appetite, reducing dietary iron intake, while tannins and polyphenols in areca nuts inhibit non-heme iron absorption and interfere with micronutrient metabolism. Additionally, arecoline generates reactive oxygen species that cause DNA damage and chromosomal instability, inhibits umbilical endothelial cell proliferation, and produces cytotoxic metabolites detectable in fetal tissues. Maternal anemia resulting from areca nut chewing reduces oxygen-carrying capacity, precipitating fetal hypoxemia and metabolic acidosis reflected in reduced neonatal APGAR scores, while chronic chewing-associated periodontal disease and oral fibrosis further compromise maternal nutrition by limiting mastication. These findings establish areca nut chewing cessation during pregnancy as a critical public health intervention to prevent adverse perinatal outcomes.

title: Effect of Xiaonang Yusi decoction (消囊育嗣汤) on IVF outcomes in patients with phlegm-dampness type PCOS: a prospective cohort study with supporting metabolomics, network pharmacology, and molecular docking analysis
summary: This study demonstrates that Xiaonang Yusi Decoction, a traditional Chinese medicine formula, significantly improves IVF outcomes in phlegm-dampness type polycystic ovary syndrome patients, with clinical pregnancy rates increasing from 29.0% to 67.9% and ongoing pregnancy rates from 29.0% to 60.7%. The therapeutic mechanism involves modulation of arachidonic acid metabolism, particularly reducing elevated levels of 15(S)-hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acid in follicular fluid, which is dysregulated in PCOS and contributes to impaired granulosa cell function, oxidative stress, and compromised oocyte competence. The formula targets the PI3K-Akt signaling pathway and modulates key regulators including ESR1 and SIRT1, while its constituent herbs work synergistically to tonify kidney yang (through Yinyanghuo and Shichangpu), resolve phlegm-dampness (through Banxia, Zhishi, and Cangzhu), clear phlegm-heat (through Danxing and Gualou), and regulate qi and blood circulation (through Chongweizi, Xiangfu, and Danshen). By normalizing arachidonic acid metabolic pathways and reducing inflammatory mediators that dysregulate the follicular microenvironment, the decoction restores granulosa cell-oocyte communication and improves embryonic development. These findings integrate metabolomic profiling and network pharmacology to provide mechanistic evidence supporting traditional Chinese medicine's effectiveness in treating PCOS-related infertility.

title: Construction of an Adverse Outcome Pathway Framework for Glyphosate-Induced Female Reproductive Toxicity Based on Toxicity Pathways
summary:
# Summary

This adverse outcome pathway framework reveals that glyphosate and glyphosate-based herbicides induce female reproductive toxicity through three interconnected mechanisms. First, glyphosate directly activates estrogen receptor α (ERα) while simultaneously inhibiting aromatase, the key enzyme for estrogen biosynthesis, creating a paradoxical hormonal dysregulation that disrupts normal follicular development, ovulation, and endometrial proliferation. Second, glyphosate impairs mitochondrial function by inhibiting electron transport chain complexes and succinate dehydrogenase, leading to reduced ATP synthesis, mitochondrial swelling, and excessive reactive oxygen species generation that is further exacerbated by zinc chelation and compromised antioxidant defenses. These oxidative and metabolic insults cascade into reproductive pathologies including impaired oocyte maturation, ovulatory dysfunction, ovarian reserve depletion, abnormal endometrial hyperplasia, and ultimately premature ovarian insufficiency and endometrial cancer. At the molecular level, glyphosate exposure triggers DNA damage, epigenetic modifications, and cell cycle arrest, with adjuvants like polyoxyethylene amine enhancing cellular uptake and potentiating toxicity even at sub-agricultural exposure levels. Epidemiological evidence supports these mechanistic findings, linking glyphosate exposure to adverse birth outcomes including preterm birth and birth defects alongside alterations in sex hormone levels.

title: Obesity and oxidative stress: potential mechanisms in endometrial disorders
summary: Obesity induces oxidative stress through multiple interconnected pathways including disrupted energy metabolism, chronic systemic inflammation, and alterations in gut microbiota composition. This obesity-induced oxidative stress causes significant damage to endometrial tissue by triggering excessive reactive oxygen species production, which damages DNA, proteins, and lipids, leading to cellular dysfunction and apoptosis. The resulting oxidative stress impairs critical endometrial functions including angiogenesis, decidualization, and endometrial receptivity—mechanisms fundamental to successful embryo implantation and fertility. Beyond endometrial effects, oxidative stress mediates obesity's broader pathological consequences, including increased risk of diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular disease, hepatic and renal dysfunction, and malignancies. These findings establish oxidative stress as a crucial mechanistic link explaining how obesity compromises endometrial structure and function, ultimately contributing to endometrial diseases and infertility in obese women. Understanding this oxidative stress pathway offers potential therapeutic targets for managing obesity-related endometrial pathology and improving reproductive outcomes.

title: The Gut–Extracellular Vesicle–Mitochondria Axis in Reproductive Aging: Antioxidant and Anti-Senescence Mechanisms
summary: Reproductive aging involves a convergence of cellular senescence, mitochondrial dysfunction, and cumulative oxidative stress that progressively impair oocyte and sperm quality. The gut microbiome emerges as a critical regulator of this process, producing metabolites including urolithin A, short-chain fatty acids, and polyphenol derivatives that enhance mitochondrial quality control and activate antioxidant pathways while suppressing senescence-associated phenotypes. Extracellular vesicles facilitate intercellular communication by transporting antioxidant enzymes, regulatory microRNAs, and bioactive lipids that regulate mitochondrial redox balance and mitophagy across reproductive tissues during gamete maturation, fertilization, embryo development, and implantation. Dysbiosis impairs these protective mechanisms by reducing glutathione synthesis and increasing reactive oxygen species generation, thereby impairing mitochondrial integrity and promoting chronic inflammation. Specific microbial metabolites drive mitochondrial rejuvenation through distinct mechanisms: urolithin A activates the PINK1–Parkin mitophagy pathway and enhances sperm motility; butyrate promotes transcription of mitochondrial regulators; short-chain fatty acids activate Nrf2 signaling; and tryptophan-derived metabolites improve mitochondrial stability via aryl hydrocarbon receptor signaling. These findings establish the gut–extracellular vesicle–mitochondria axis as a targetable mechanism for mitigating age-related reproductive decline through microbiota-directed interventions.

title: Oxidative Stress and Inflammatory Biomarkers in Male Infertility: A Narrative Review of Diagnostic Value and Clinical Integration
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress and inflammation form a self-amplifying pathological cycle in male infertility, wherein excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) damage sperm through lipid peroxidation, protein oxidation, mitochondrial dysfunction, and DNA fragmentation, ultimately reducing sperm motility and viability. Pro-inflammatory cytokines—including interleukin-6, interleukin-8, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and interferon-gamma—accumulate in the seminal plasma of infertile men and exacerbate oxidative damage by recruiting leukocytes while simultaneously impairing steroidogenesis. Several biomarkers demonstrate clinical utility in diagnosing oxidative stress-related male infertility, including malondialdehyde (lipid peroxidation marker), 8-hydroxy-2′-deoxyguanosine (oxidative DNA damage marker), and total antioxidant capacity, which collectively reflect the degree of oxidative imbalance. Oxidative stress and inflammation are associated with multiple semen abnormalities—oligozoospermia, asthenozoospermia, teratozoospermia, and elevated sperm DNA fragmentation—while pathological conditions such as varicocele, diabetes mellitus, and metabolic syndrome, alongside lifestyle factors including smoking, alcohol consumption, and obesity, sustain chronic oxidative imbalance. Genital infections such as prostatitis trigger localized inflammatory responses producing leukocytospermia and elevated cytokine levels, further reinforcing the oxidative-inflammatory vicious loop that characterizes male reproductive dysfunction.

title: Effects of Dexmedetomidine Treatment After Cerebral Ischemia/Reperfusion on Apoptosis and Oxidative Stress: A Rat Model
summary:
# Summary

Cerebral ischemia/reperfusion injury triggers excessive oxidative stress and apoptotic cell death through the generation of reactive oxygen species, inflammatory cytokine release, mitochondrial dysfunction, and lipid peroxidation, while simultaneously depleting endogenous antioxidant enzymes including superoxide dismutase, catalase, and glutathione peroxidase. Dexmedetomidine treatment provides neuroprotection by restoring antioxidant capacity, reducing oxidative stress markers (malondialdehyde and total oxidant status), and suppressing apoptotic pathways through modulation of the pro-apoptotic Bax/Bcl-2 balance and downstream effectors like APAF-1 and caspase-3. The drug's protective mechanisms involve coordinated activation of multiple survival signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt/mTOR, JAK/STAT3, MAPK, and HIF-1α/p53, alongside upregulation of antioxidant proteins such as thioredoxin-1. Dexmedetomidine stabilizes mitochondrial function and prevents neuronal degeneration and edema following ischemic injury, demonstrating broad efficacy in counteracting the multifaceted cellular damage induced by cerebral ischemia/reperfusion.

title: Purified Zearalenone at the Regulatory Limit Exhibits No Overt Toxicity in Broilers
summary: Zearalenone (ZEA), a non-steroidal estrogenic mycotoxin produced by Fusarium species, exerts its primary toxicological effects by competitively binding to estrogen receptors and activating estrogen response elements, disrupting endocrine homeostasis and generating excessive reactive oxygen species that compromise cellular antioxidant defenses. At the regulatory limit of 4 mg/kg in broilers, ZEA causes dose-dependent immunological and oxidative stress responses, including altered serum markers of antioxidant capacity (superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase) and inflammatory cytokines (interleukins 2, 4, and 10), with tissue-specific effects on intestinal morphology characterized by villus atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and mucosal epithelial exfoliation. At higher doses, ZEA induces severe pathological changes including pulmonary secretory cell metaplasia and renal tubular degeneration with necrosis. The mycotoxin also significantly disrupts the jejunal microbiota composition, reducing overall microbial diversity and species richness while increasing Firmicutes and Lactobacillus abundance at the expense of beneficial bacteria including Proteobacteria, Bacteroidota, and short-chain fatty acid-producing families. These multisystem effects demonstrate that despite being administered at regulatory limits, ZEA exhibits measurable toxic impacts on antioxidant systems, immune function, intestinal integrity, and microbial ecology in poultry.

title: Role of antioxidant vitamins in mitigating the health risks from environmental toxicant exposures: a narrative review
summary: Environmental toxicants including persistent organic pollutants, heavy metals, and fine particulate matter cause oxidative stress and cellular damage by increasing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production, which subsequently damages lipids, proteins, and DNA through lipid peroxidation, protein oxidation, and DNA lesions. Antioxidant vitamins—including vitamins A, C, and E—mitigate these effects through complementary mechanisms: vitamin E scavenges peroxyl radicals in lipid-rich cellular membranes, vitamin C directly scavenges free radicals in aqueous phases while inhibiting NADPH oxidase and synergizing with vitamin E, and vitamin A scavenges peroxyl radicals and prevents oxidative damage. In studies of specific toxicants such as bisphenol A, phthalates, and pesticides, antioxidant vitamins demonstrated protective effects on reproductive organs and tissue function, with vitamin combinations showing enhanced protective efficacy against genotoxicity and organ damage. Environmental chemical exposure depletes endogenous plasma levels of these protective vitamins, underscoring the clinical relevance of supplementation as a practical intervention strategy. Collectively, the evidence supports antioxidant vitamins as a promising protective approach to mitigate the oxidative stress-mediated health risks from widespread environmental toxicant exposures.

title: Epididymal macrophage senescence contributes to sperm motility decrease upon environmental stress
summary:
# Summary

Environmental heavy metals including lead, cadmium, and mercury induce epididymal macrophage senescence, which directly contributes to reduced sperm motility and male infertility. The underlying mechanism involves environmental stress increasing N6-methyladenosine (m6A) modifications on Foxo3 mRNA through elevated methyltransferase activity (METTL14) and reduced demethylase activity (FTO), thereby suppressing the antiaging gene Foxo3 and promoting senescent macrophage activation with elevated p16, p21, and IL-6 expression. Senescent epididymal macrophages produce inflammatory senescence-associated secretory phenotypes that impair sperm function and disrupt the epididymal microenvironment. Two therapeutic approaches successfully restored sperm motility by reversing macrophage senescence: FTO overexpression and S-adenosylhomocysteine supplementation, both of which reduced m6A methylation levels, restored FOXO3 expression, and downregulated senescence markers. These findings establish FTO-mediated Foxo3 m6A modification as a critical regulator of epididymal macrophage senescence and identify novel targets for alleviating environmental stress-induced male infertility.

title: Endometrial Cell Senescence and Recurrent Spontaneous Abortion: Biomarker Potential of UCP2 and GSR
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies endometrial cell senescence as a critical mechanism underlying recurrent spontaneous abortion (RSA), with uncoupling protein 2 (UCP2) and glutathione reductase (GSR) emerging as promising biomarkers. Oxidative stress, particularly hydrogen peroxide-induced damage, drives endometrial senescence by upregulating markers p16^INK4a and p21, which subsequently promotes senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP) factors that disrupt the endometrial microenvironment and impair embryo implantation. UCP2 and GSR show strong diagnostic efficacy (AUC ≥ 0.77) and are significantly upregulated in RSA patients, where they associate with immune dysregulation characterized by elevated activated NK cells and CD8+ T cells while reduced M2 macrophages indicate a pro-inflammatory state. Mechanistically, GSR reduces oxidative stress through glutathione reduction while UCP2 regulates mitochondrial ROS production, and both proteins correlate with immune cell populations that contribute to miscarriage risk. Drug prediction analyses identified dexamethasone and menadione as potential therapeutic modulators of UCP2 and GSR expression, suggesting targeted interventions to restore endometrial health. These findings establish oxidative stress-induced senescence and immune dysregulation as interconnected pathogenic pathways in RSA, with UCP2 and GSR serving as both mechanistic insights and clinical biomarkers for intervention.

title: Global Burden of Lead Exposure: Integrating Epidemiology, Molecular Mechanisms, and Mitigation Approaches
summary:
# Summary

Lead exposure causes widespread toxicity through multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on oxidative stress and metal homeostasis disruption. Lead increases reactive oxygen species while simultaneously inhibiting critical antioxidant enzymes (SOD, CAT, GPx) and depleting glutathione, creating severe oxidative stress that damages DNA, proteins, and lipids. At the molecular level, lead disrupts calcium signaling, impairs heme synthesis, triggers mitochondrial dysfunction and endoplasmic reticulum stress, and activates NADPH oxidase in endothelial and immune cells. Chronic lead exposure causes dose-dependent cognitive impairment (documented at blood lead levels as low as 2.5 µg/dL), alongside systemic effects including anemia, hypertension, renal dysfunction, reproductive harm, and peripheral neuropathy, with children absorbing 40–50% of ingested lead compared to 10–15% in adults. Lead bioaccumulates primarily in bone (70% of body burden) but distributes to brain, liver, and kidneys while crossing the placenta to expose fetuses, and sex differences in susceptibility exist due to estrogen-facilitated excretion in premenopausal women. The WHO estimates lead exposure accounted for nearly half of over 2 million chemical-related deaths globally in 2019, representing a major public health burden preventable through environmental mitigation and exposure reduction strategies.

title: Administration of N-acetylcysteine influence the expression of apoptotic genes in the granulosa cells of infertile women diagnosed with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis-related infertility is driven by excessive reactive oxygen species and oxidative stress, which trigger apoptosis in granulosa cells through upregulation of pro-apoptotic genes Bax and Caspase-3 while suppressing the anti-apoptotic gene Bcl-2. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) treatment counteracts this pathological cascade by reducing oxidative stress and increasing serum antioxidant capacity (SOD and total antioxidant capacity), thereby restoring the balance of apoptotic gene expression in granulosa cells. In infertile women with endometriosis, NAC administration significantly downregulated Bax and Caspase-3 expression while upregulating Bcl-2, effectively reducing granulosa cell apoptosis and improving oocyte quality and embryo development. These findings demonstrate that NAC's antioxidant and anti-apoptotic effects provide a therapeutic mechanism to preserve granulosa cell function and enhance fertility outcomes in endometriosis patients experiencing oxidative stress-induced infertility.

title: Mesenchymal stem cell therapy for premature ovarian insufficiency: progress and challenges
summary: Mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapy represents a promising treatment for premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), a condition affecting 1–3% of women under 40 that can result from chemotherapy, genetic factors, autoimmunity, or environmental toxins. MSCs derived from bone marrow, adipose tissue, umbilical cord, or menstrual blood secrete growth factors (IGF-1, HGF, VEGF) and exosomes enriched in regulatory microRNAs (miR-21, miR-126) that collectively inhibit granulosa cell apoptosis, reduce oxidative stress, and promote angiogenesis through activation of the PI3K–Akt survival pathway. The cytoprotective mechanisms involve miR-21-mediated downregulation of apoptotic regulators (PTEN, PDCD4) and mitigation of the senescence-associated secretory phenotype that characterizes post-chemotherapy ovarian damage. Preclinical studies demonstrate that intra-ovarian MSC transplantation restores follicular reserves and hormonal homeostasis, while countering chemotherapy-induced oxidative stress and fibrosis that drive follicular loss through classical apoptosis, necroptosis, and ferroptosis pathways. However, clinical translation faces challenges including optimizing MSC source, persistence, and immunomodulation, understanding long-term safety particularly regarding potential enhancement of ovarian cancer metastasis by cancer-associated MSCs, and clarifying whether MSCs can reverse dysregulation of the PI3K–Akt–FOXO3 axis underlying premature follicle activation in POI.

title: Interaction Between Composite Dietary Antioxidant Index and Alcohol Consumption on Cardiovascular Diseases: NHANES 2005–2018
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how the Composite Dietary Antioxidant Index (CDAI) and alcohol consumption jointly influence cardiovascular disease risk using NHANES data from 2005–2018. The research demonstrates that higher CDAI levels, reflecting greater dietary intake of antioxidants (vitamins A, C, E, zinc, selenium, and carotenoids), are associated with significantly lower cardiovascular disease prevalence. A critical interaction was identified wherein high CDAI combined with moderate alcohol consumption provides the greatest cardioprotective effect, while low CDAI paired with never drinking substantially increases CVD risk. The cardioprotective mechanisms involve dietary antioxidants reducing oxidative stress and its downstream pathological cascades—including endothelial dysfunction, inflammation via NF-κB pathway activation, and vascular smooth muscle cell proliferation and migration. These findings suggest that dietary antioxidant sufficiency is particularly important for cardiovascular health, with moderate alcohol consumption providing additional benefits, whereas antioxidant-poor diets confer increased CVD risk independent of alcohol consumption patterns.

title: Preventing ovarian aging: from redox-targeted strategies to extracellular vesicle-based therapies
summary: Ovarian aging is a multifactorial process driven by oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and chronic inflammation, which are triggered by environmental, dietary, and metabolic factors including smoking, high-fat diets, endocrine-disrupting chemicals, and micro- and nanoplastics. These mechanisms converge to activate inflamm-aging, characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory mediators such as IL-6 and TNF-α that promote follicular atresia and suppress steroidogenesis, ultimately leading to premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) with consequences extending to cardiovascular, skeletal, and cognitive health. Elevated reactive oxygen species directly injure oocytes and granulosa cells, accelerating follicular depletion and senescence, whereby senescent ovarian cells release a pro-inflammatory secretome that perpetuates ovarian dysfunction through paracrine mechanisms. Current therapeutic approaches include antioxidant-based strategies such as melatonin, N-acetylcysteine, coenzyme Q10, polyphenols, and vitamins C and E, complemented by emerging stem cell-derived extracellular vesicle therapies that reduce oxidative stress and support mitochondrial function. This integrated understanding positions ovarian aging as a potentially modifiable process amenable to targeted redox and cell-based interventions.

title: Exploratory pilot trial of astaxanthin supplementation in PCOS patients at risk of OHSS with focus on RAGE–NFκB pathway
summary:
# Summary

This pilot trial demonstrates that astaxanthin supplementation modulates the AGE-RAGE-NFκB inflammatory pathway in PCOS patients at high risk for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS). In PCOS, hyperandrogenism upregulates RAGE expression, enabling advanced glycation end products to activate NFκB signaling, which drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production and VEGF secretion—key mediators of OHSS pathogenesis characterized by excessive vascular permeability. Astaxanthin treatment significantly reduced RAGE mRNA expression, suppressed NFκB pathway activation (evidenced by lower pIκB/IκB ratios), and decreased interleukin-6 levels in granulosa cells and follicular fluid. Most notably, astaxanthin supplementation substantially improved oocyte maturity rates (71.5% versus 61.2% in placebo), addressing a major problem in PCOS patients who often experience diminished oocyte quality despite high follicle numbers. Although OHSS incidence showed only a numerical trend toward reduction, the combination of improved oocyte maturation with molecular evidence of reduced RAGE signaling suggests astaxanthin may simultaneously enhance reproductive outcomes while mitigating OHSS risk through antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms.

title: Cell-free mitochondrial DNA (cf-mtDNA) in human body fluids: molecular characteristics, release mechanisms, and clinical translation—an updated review
summary:
# Summary

Cell-free mitochondrial DNA (cf-mtDNA) has emerged as a promising non-invasive molecular biomarker for disease diagnosis and prognosis across multiple conditions, including diabetes, cancer, systemic lupus erythematosus, acute kidney injury, and embryo quality assessment. Released from damaged tissues or actively secreted by cells, cf-mtDNA circulates either as free nucleic acid or within extracellular vesicles such as exosomes and microvesicles, with cellular stress and mitochondrial dysfunction serving as primary drivers of its release through mechanisms including the mitochondrial permeability transition pore and mitophagy. Once in circulation, cf-mtDNA functions as a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) that binds Toll-like receptors, activating NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways to trigger caspase-1 activation and proinflammatory cytokine release, thereby promoting sterile inflammation and tissue injury. Oxidative stress-mediated mtDNA damage initiates a pathological cycle of reactive oxygen species production and mitochondrial impairment, linking cf-mtDNA elevation to multiple chronic diseases. Notably, interventions such as moderate-intensity exercise and chronic endurance training reduce cf-mtDNA levels through improved mitochondrial quality control, suggesting therapeutic potential for mitigating cf-mtDNA-associated pathology in various disease contexts.

title: Reactive oxygen species in fetal growth restriction mechanisms and therapeutic directions (Review)
summary:
# Summary

Placental oxidative stress is a central pathogenic mechanism in fetal growth restriction, characterized by elevated biomarkers including malondialdehyde, 4-hydroxynonenal, reactive oxygen metabolites, and 8-hydroxy-2'-deoxyguanosine in maternal plasma, cord blood, and placental tissues. While moderate oxidative stress is essential for early placental function, trophoblast invasion, and angiogenesis, excessive oxidative stress disrupts this balance through multiple interconnected mechanisms including endoplasmic reticulum stress, metabolic reprogramming, and epigenetic alterations that ultimately lead to trophoblast dysfunction and placental vascular injury. The underlying pathophysiology involves impaired spiral artery remodeling, which triggers hypoxia-reoxygenation cycles that activate NADPH oxidase and xanthine oxidase to generate excessive reactive oxygen species, causing DNA and protein damage while antioxidant defenses become dysregulated. Dysregulation of microRNAs (miR-199a, miR-210-3p, miR-21) and epigenetic alterations including DNA hypomethylation and histone modifications further contribute to fetal growth restriction pathogenesis. Emerging therapeutic approaches include melatonin, pentoxifylline, and mitochondria-targeted antioxidants such as mitoquinone mesylate, as well as classical antioxidants like N-acetylcysteine, tempol, and resveratrol, which show promise in preclinical investigations for ameliorating placental oxidative injury and improving fetal outcomes.

title: Association of Smoking and Alcohol Consumption with Female Reproductive Diseases: A Mendelian Randomization Study on the Mediating Role of Circulating Metabolites
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study establishes causal relationships between smoking and alcohol consumption with female reproductive diseases, with circulating metabolites serving as key mediators. Genetic predisposition to smoking initiation causally increases risk of abnormal uterine bleeding, endometrioid ovarian cancer, ovarian cysts, and pelvic inflammatory diseases, while cigarette consumption per day directly elevates adenomyosis risk with glycoprotein acetyls and VLDL subtype markers mediating approximately 2.76–7.61% of this effect. Mechanistically, smoking induces systemic metabolic remodeling by activating the tryptophan–kynurenine pathway and sphingolipid networks, while nicotine and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons promote oxidative stress, apoptosis, and aryl hydrocarbon receptor activation in reproductive tissues, ultimately inhibiting estrogen production. Elevated acylcarnitine levels, a hallmark metabolic feature of endometriosis, reflect impaired fatty acid oxidation from smoking exposure, with triglycerides mediating 1.08–1.77% of smoking's effect on pelvic inflammatory diseases. These findings identify metabolic dysregulation as a clinically relevant therapeutic target and provide molecular evidence that smoking cessation interventions could reduce gynecological disease risk through restoration of normal metabolic pathways.

title: The association between dominant food patterns and the intake of energy and macronutrients with endometriosis in women aged 15–45
summary:
# Summary

This study identified dietary patterns as significant modulators of endometriosis risk in women aged 15–45 through their effects on inflammation, estrogen, and metabolic dysregulation. Diets high in red meat, hydrogenated fats, and high-glycemic foods strongly associate with increased endometriosis risk, operating through multiple interconnected mechanisms: saturated fatty acids and palmitic acid promote lipopolysaccharide production, which activates the TLR4/NF-κB inflammatory pathway, while simultaneously elevating endogenous estrogen through reduced sex hormone-binding globulin and hyperinsulinemia-induced pathways. Conversely, protective dietary components including antioxidants (Myo-inositol and Alpha-lipoic acid), omega-3 fatty acids, and dairy products containing calcium and vitamin D reduce oxidative stress, improve insulin sensitivity, and lower growth factors and inflammation. The study demonstrates that dietary interventions targeting reduction of proinflammatory nutrients and increased consumption of anti-inflammatory foods represent a critical therapeutic strategy for mitigating endometriosis-related infertility and pelvic pain in reproductive-aged women.

title: HIF-1α mediated placental ischemic signaling in the development of early-onset preeclampsia
summary:
# Summary

Early-onset preeclampsia is driven by a pathogenic cascade initiated by placental ischemia and sustained hypoxia, which activates HIF-1α as a central regulator. Under persistent hypoxic conditions, HIF-1α shifts from its normal developmental role—promoting early trophoblast proliferation and differentiation—to a pathological program that suppresses trophoblast invasion and extravillous differentiation, thereby perpetuating placental ischemia through a self-reinforcing feedback loop. This sustained HIF-1α activation simultaneously drives mitochondrial dysfunction, upregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α) and antiangiogenic factors (sFlt-1), while suppressing pro-angiogenic signals (VEGF), leading to severe systemic angiogenic imbalance and endothelial dysfunction. Elevated circulating sFlt-1 neutralizes VEGF and PLGF signaling in maternal endothelial cells, precipitating the clinical manifestations of preeclampsia including hypertension, proteinuria, organ damage, and fetal growth restriction. Hypoxia-reoxygenation cycles additionally generate reactive oxygen species that damage cellular lipids, proteins, and mitochondrial DNA, compounding placental injury and amplifying the stress response. Pre-existing maternal vascular dysfunction from chronic diseases or environmental exposures (smoking, nutritional deficiency) increases susceptibility by impairing the normal oxygen-dependent developmental transition required for adequate spiral artery remodeling.

title: Rhythms of life: melatonin, nutrition, sleep, and antioxidant strategies for healthy aging
summary:
# Summary

Melatonin serves as a central integrative signal regulating circadian rhythm synchrony, sleep quality, and antioxidant defense mechanisms. Age-related declines in melatonin secretion and signaling drive accelerated aging through increased oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and inflammaging, while also impairing circadian homeostasis and promoting cellular senescence. The suprachiasmatic nucleus governs melatonin secretion in response to light cues, and proper nighttime melatonin signaling synchronizes peripheral clocks in metabolic tissues including the liver, pancreas, and adipose tissue. Circadian misalignment—whether from aging, late eating, or bright light exposure—is associated with metabolic dysfunction, neurodegeneration, and disease acceleration. Restoration of melatonin levels through dietary sources of melatonin and its precursor tryptophan, combined with consumption of antioxidant-rich polyphenol-containing foods and time-restricted feeding practices, can mitigate oxidative stress and reinforce circadian robustness. These findings underscore melatonin as both a biomarker and mediator of healthy aging, highlighting nutritional and behavioral strategies to maintain circadian synchrony and reduce age-related pathology.

title: Cell-free DNA in female infertility: from pathological mechanisms to clinical biomarkers
summary:
# Summary

Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) functions as a critical pathological mediator in endometriosis-related infertility by driving a cascade of inflammation, oxidative stress, and neutrophil extracellular trap (NET) formation. The inflammatory microenvironment in endometriosis enhances cellular apoptosis, elevating serum cfDNA levels, particularly mitochondrial-derived cfDNA (cf-mtDNA), which activates Toll-like receptor 9 and downstream NF-κB and MAPK signaling pathways to promote pro-inflammatory cytokine release. This chronic inflammation impairs endometrial receptivity and stromal cell differentiation, while elevated NETs perpetuate inflammation via TLR9, collectively disrupting folliculogenesis and accelerating follicular atresia in the ovary. Elevated cfDNA long-fragment ratios serve as biomarkers of chronic inflammation in endometriosis and premature ovarian insufficiency, and reduced cfDNA integrity predicts poor embryo quality in assisted reproductive technology. Additionally, cfDNA fragmentation patterns in spent embryo culture medium correlate with embryo developmental potential, offering non-invasive genetic screening applications for improving fertility outcomes in endometriosis and related ovulatory disorders.

title: The consequences of the extremely low frequency electromagnetic field (ELF-EMF) exposure on physiological processes in the uterus: novel insights and implications
summary:
# Summary

Extremely low frequency electromagnetic field (ELF-EMF) exposure induces widespread disruption of uterine physiology through multiple interconnected mechanisms. ELF-EMF alters the endometrial and myometrial transcriptomic landscape by modulating epigenetic processes including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and microRNA biogenesis, which collectively dysregulate expression of genes involved in cell proliferation, apoptosis, and oxidative stress responses. The exposure disrupts critical uterine functions by interfering with calcium ion channel regulation, which compromises uterine contractility, secretory activity, and hormonal signaling, while simultaneously affecting steroidogenesis in both myometrial and endometrial tissues. ELF-EMF elevates reactive oxygen species production, triggering oxidative stress that damages DNA and initiates signaling cascades altering gene expression of apoptotic regulators (CASP3, CASP7), proliferation factors (Cyclin D2, TP53, BCL2), and immune modulators (TNF, IL15). These molecular alterations ultimately compromise endometrial receptivity and uterine function, with particular implications for implantation and reproductive competence relevant to endometriosis-associated infertility.
